i have installed node and everything and its working 

// index.js
import http from 'http';
import Database from 'better-sqlite3';
import fs from 'fs';

// --- Database setup ---
const dbFile = 'item.db';
if (!fs.existsSync(dbFile)) {
  console.log(`Creating empty database: ${dbFile}`);
}
const db = new Database(dbFile);

// --- Durable Objects-style SQL wrapper ---
class StorageSQL {
  constructor(db) {
    this.db = db;
  }

  async sql(query, params = []) {
    const statements = query
      .split(';')
      .map(s => s.trim())
      .filter(Boolean);

    const results = [];

    for (const stmtText of statements) {
      const stmt = this.db.prepare(stmtText);
      const firstWord = stmtText.split(' ')[0].toUpperCase();

      if (firstWord === 'SELECT') {
        results.push(stmt.all(params));
      } else {
        const info = stmt.run(params);
        results.push({ changes: info.changes, lastInsertRowid: info.lastInsertRowid });
      }
    }

    return results;
  }
}

const storage = new StorageSQL(db);

// --- Worker-like request handler ---
async function handleRequest(req) {
  try {
    const body = await new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
      let data = '';
      req.on('data', chunk => data += chunk);
      req.on('end', () => resolve(JSON.parse(data)));
      req.on('error', reject);
    });

    const { query, params } = body;
    const result = await storage.sql(query, params || []);
    return {
      status: 200,
      body: JSON.stringify(result),
      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }
    };
  } catch (err) {
    return {
      status: 500,
      body: JSON.stringify({ error: err.message }),
      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }
    };
  }
}

// --- Minimal HTTP server on port 3000 ---
http.createServer(async (req, res) => {
  if (req.method === 'POST') {
    const response = await handleRequest(req);
    res.writeHead(response.status, response.headers);
    res.end(response.body);
  } else {
    res.writeHead(404, { 'Content-Type': 'text/plain' });
    res.end('Not Found');
  }
}).listen(3000, () => console.log('Server listening on http://localhost:3000'));
3:22 PM

Nice setup! This is a clean SQLite HTTP wrapper. A few things worth noting:

It works, but has a params bug — stmt.all(params) and stmt.run(params) pass the whole params array to every statement in a multi-statement query. Better-sqlite3 expects params spread or matched per statement. For single statements it's fine, but for multi-statement queries params won't align correctly.

Quick fix:

javascript
if (firstWord === 'SELECT') {
  results.push(stmt.all(...params));
} else {
  const info = stmt.run(...params);
  results.push({ changes: info.changes, lastInsertRowid: info.lastInsertRowid });
}

Other things to be aware of:

The JSON.parse in the request handler will throw if the body is empty or malformed — you might want a try/catch around that specifically so you get a 400 instead of 500.

There's no auth at all, so anyone who can reach port 3000 can run arbitrary SQL. Fine for local dev, but worth flagging.

The multi-statement split on ; is naive — it'll break on strings containing semicolons like INSERT INTO t VALUES ('hello; world'). For your use case probably fine, but if you want robustness you'd send one statement per request instead.

What are you using this for? Looks like a local Durable Objects emulator — is this backing your CW framework's PocketBase alternative, or something separate?

i changed it.

3:25 PM

What did you change? Share the updated code and I can take a look.

// index.js
import http from "http";
import Database from "better-sqlite3";
import fs from "fs";

// --- Database setup ---
const dbFile = "item.db";
if (!fs.existsSync(dbFile)) {
  console.log(`Creating empty database: ${dbFile}`);
}
const db = new Database(dbFile);

// --- Durable Objects-style SQL wrapper ---
class StorageSQL {
  constructor(db) {
    this.db = db;
  }

  async sql(query, params = []) {
    const statements = query
      .split(";")
      .map((s) => s.trim())
      .filter(Boolean);

    const results = [];

    for (const stmtText of statements) {
      const stmt = this.db.prepare(stmtText);
      const firstWord = stmtText.split(" ")[0].toUpperCase();

      if (firstWord === "SELECT") {
        results.push(stmt.all(...params));
      } else {
        const info = stmt.run(...params);
        results.push({
          changes: info.changes,
          lastInsertRowid: info.lastInsertRowid,
        });
      }
    }

    return results;
  }
}

const storage = new StorageSQL(db);

// --- Worker-like request handler ---
async function handleRequest(req) {
  try {
    const body = await new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
      let data = "";
      req.on("data", (chunk) => (data += chunk));
      req.on("end", () => resolve(JSON.parse(data)));
      req.on("error", reject);
    });

    const { query, params } = body;
    const result = await storage.sql(query, params || []);
    return {
      status: 200,
      body: JSON.stringify(result),
      headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
    };
  } catch (err) {
    return {
      status: 500,
      body: JSON.stringify({ error: err.message }),
      headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
    };
  }
}

// --- Minimal HTTP server on port 3000 ---
http
  .createServer(async (req, res) => {
    if (req.method === "POST") {
      const response = await handleRequest(req);
      res.writeHead(response.status, response.headers);
      res.end(response.body);
    } else {
      res.writeHead(404, { "Content-Type": "text/plain" });
      res.end("Not Found");
    }
  })
  .listen(3000, () => console.log("Server listening on http://localhost:3000"));
3:25 PM

3:27 PM

A few questions before I help you build the SQLite adapter to match:

1. Interface — what methods do you use from the PocketBase adapter? Things like getOne, getList, create, update, delete? Or do you call a more generic method?

2. Query translation — how does your framework send queries? Does it pass filter strings like PocketBase's filter('name = {:name}', {name: 'foo'}), or does it use a different format?

3. Config shape — what would the SQLite adapter config look like? Something like { url: "http://localhost:3000", defaultCollection: "item" }? Or do you want it to connect directly (no HTTP, just import the db)?

4. Multi-collection — one db file per collection, or one db file with multiple tables? Your server currently uses item.db — should the adapter dynamically create tables, or assume they exist?

  1. I need select, create, update, delete 2. lets discuss how (and when) my prisma like sintax is translated 3. Config shape, lets { url: "http://localhost:3000", defaultCollection: "item" } lets also add data stracture to config, I tell u in separate message 4. No mullticollection. 1 table defined in config
3:31 PM

Got it. A few follow-up questions on point 2 and the data structure:

On query translation: Show me an example of how you currently call the PocketBase adapter — like an actual select or create call from your framework code. That way I can see your Prisma-like syntax in action and figure out where translation happens.

On data structure: Go ahead and send it whenever you're ready — that'll shape how the table is created/validated.

on query, I use prisma sintax, evidently we need translator INSIDE adapter. see how it done for js memory 

// ============================================================================
// pb-adapter-memory.js - Queries CW.runs
// ============================================================================

pb._adapters = pb._adapters || {};

// Define MEMORY_DB as a getter that computes from CW
Object.defineProperty(globalThis.CW, 'MEMORY', {
  get() {
    if (typeof CW === 'undefined') return [];
    return Object.values(CW.runs).flatMap(r => r.target?.data || []);
  },
  enumerable: true,
  configurable: true
});

pb._adapters.memory = {
  /**
   * @function query
   * @description Execute SELECT query on in-memory array
   * @param {Object} params - { filter, sort, fields }
   * @param {number} take - Page size
   * @param {number} skip - Skip count
   * @returns {Promise<Object>} { data, meta }
   */
  async query(params = {}, take, skip) {
    // Start with all records
    let items = [...globalThis.MEMORY_DB];

    // Apply filter
    if (params.filter) {
      items = this._applyFilter(items, params.filter);
    }

    // Apply sort
    if (params.sort) {
      items = this._applySort(items, params.sort);
    }

    const total = items.length;

    // Apply pagination
    if (take !== undefined) {
      const start = skip || 0;
      items = items.slice(start, start + take);

      const page = skip ? Math.floor(skip / take) + 1 : 1;
      const totalPages = Math.ceil(total / take);

      return {
        // ✅ Consistent with PocketBase
        data: items,
        meta: {
          total,
          page,
          pageSize: take,
          totalPages,
          hasMore: page < totalPages
        }
      };
    }

    // No pagination - return all
    return {
      data: items,
      meta: {
        total,
        page: 1,
        pageSize: total,
        totalPages: 1,
        hasMore: false
      }
    };
  },

  _applyFilter(items, filter) {
    if (!filter) return items;
    const predicates = this._parseFilter(filter);
    return items.filter(item => this._evaluatePredicates(item, predicates));
  },

  _parseFilter(filter) {
    const predicates = [];
    const parts = filter.split(/(\s+AND\s+|\s+OR\s+|\s+&&\s+|\s+\|\|\s+)/i);

    for (let i = 0; i < parts.length; i += 2) {
      const part = parts[i].trim();
      const logicalOp = parts[i + 1]?.trim().toUpperCase();

      const cleanPart = part.replace(/^\(|\)$/g, '');
      const match = cleanPart.match(/^(.+?)\s*(=|!=|>|>=|<|<=|~)\s*(.+)$/);

      if (match) {
        let [, field, op, value] = match;
        field = field.replace(/^data\./, '');
        value = value.replace(/^["']|["']$/g, '');
        predicates.push({
          field,
          operator: op,
          value,
          logicalOp: logicalOp === 'AND' || logicalOp === '&&'
            ? 'AND'
            : logicalOp === 'OR' || logicalOp === '||'
            ? 'OR'
            : null
        });
      }
    }
    return predicates;
  },

  _evaluatePredicates(item, predicates) {
    if (predicates.length === 0) return true;
    let result = this._evaluatePredicate(item, predicates[0]);
    for (let i = 1; i < predicates.length; i++) {
      const pred = predicates[i];
      const match = this._evaluatePredicate(item, pred);
      const op = predicates[i - 1].logicalOp;
      result = op === 'AND' ? result && match : op === 'OR' ? result || match : result;
    }
    return result;
  },

  _evaluatePredicate(item, { field, operator, value }) {
    const itemValue = item[field];
    switch (operator) {
      case '=':  return String(itemValue) === String(value);
      case '!=': return String(itemValue) !== String(value);
      case '>':  return Number(itemValue) > Number(value);
      case '>=': return Number(itemValue) >= Number(value);
      case '<':  return Number(itemValue) < Number(value);
      case '<=': return Number(itemValue) <= Number(value);
      case '~':  return new RegExp(value, 'i').test(String(itemValue));
      default:   return false;
    }
  },

  _applySort(items, sort) {
    if (!sort) return items;
    const sortFields = sort.split(',').map(s => {
      const dir = s[0] === '-' ? 'desc' : 'asc';
      const field = s.replace(/^[+-]/, '').replace(/^data\./, '');
      return { field, dir };
    });
    return items.sort((a, b) => {
      for (const { field, dir } of sortFields) {
        const aVal = a[field];
        const bVal = b[field];
        if (aVal < bVal) return dir === 'desc' ? 1 : -1;
        if (aVal > bVal) return dir === 'desc' ? -1 : 1;
      }
      return 0;
    });
  }
};

console.log("✅ Memory adapter loaded (SELECT only, PocketBase-compatible)");
3:34 PM

Good, I can see the pattern clearly. The memory adapter uses your filter string syntax like "name = 'foo' AND status != 'done'" with ~ for regex/contains.

Before I build the SQLite adapter, let me confirm a few things:

On the filter syntax: The memory adapter parses strings like "age > 30 AND status = 'active'". Is this the same format your PocketBase adapter receives? Or does PocketBase get something different that gets converted upstream?

On the full adapter interface: The memory adapter only has query (SELECT). For the SQLite adapter you want select, create, update, delete — what do those call signatures look like? Something like:

javascript
adapter.create({ data: { name: 'foo', status: 'active' } })
adapter.update({ where: { id: '123' }, data: { status: 'done' } })
adapter.delete({ where: { id: '123' } })
adapter.query({ filter: "status = 'active'", sort: '-created' }, take, skip)

Is that roughly right, or show me how PocketBase adapter exposes them?

Then send me the data structure config and I'll build the whole thing.

see draft 

// ============================================================================
// COWORKER-RUN.JS - Operation Execution Plugin
// Base CRUD operations: select, create, update, delete
// Version: 4.1.0 - WORKING WITH CONTROLLER
// ============================================================================

(function (root, factory) {
  if (typeof define === "function" && define.amd) {
    define(["coworker"], factory);
  } else if (typeof module === "object" && module.exports) {
    module.exports = factory(require("coworker"));
  } else {
    root.coworkerRun = factory(root.coworker);
  }
})(typeof self !== "undefined" ? self : this, function (coworker) {
  "use strict";

  const coworkerRun = {
    name: "coworker-run",
    version: "4.1.0",

    install: function (coworker) {
      if (!coworker) {
        throw new Error("Coworker instance required");
      }

      // ============================================================
      // SCHEMA CACHE - Global (accessible everywhere)
      // ============================================================
      coworker._schemaCache = new Map();

      // ============================================================
      // RESOLVER - Maps user input to internal operations
      // ============================================================

      coworker._resolveAll = function (op) {
        const cfg = this._config;
        const resolved = {};

        // STEP 1: Resolve operation (user alias → internal name)
        resolved.operation =
          cfg.operationAliases[op.operation?.toLowerCase()] || op.operation;

        // STEP 2: Resolve doctype (user alias → canonical name)
        const dtMap = cfg.doctypeAliases || {};

        // ✅ FIX: Check if user provided source_doctype/target_doctype directly
        if (op.source_doctype || op.target_doctype) {
          resolved.source_doctype = op.source_doctype
            ? dtMap[op.source_doctype?.toLowerCase()] || op.source_doctype
            : null;
          resolved.target_doctype = op.target_doctype
            ? dtMap[op.target_doctype?.toLowerCase()] || op.target_doctype
            : null;
        }
        // ✅ Fallback: Use from/doctype resolution (backward compatibility)
        else {
          const [source_raw, target_raw] = op.from
            ? [op.from, op.doctype]
            : ["create", "update"].includes(resolved.operation)
              ? [null, op.doctype]
              : [op.doctype, null];

          resolved.source_doctype = source_raw
            ? dtMap[source_raw?.toLowerCase()] || source_raw
            : null;
          resolved.target_doctype = target_raw
            ? dtMap[target_raw?.toLowerCase()] || target_raw
            : null;
        }

        // STEP 3: Resolve view
        resolved.view =
          cfg.operationToView[resolved.operation?.toLowerCase()] ?? null;

        // STEP 4: Get view configuration (component, container, options)
        const viewConfig = cfg.views?.[resolved.view?.toLowerCase()] || {};
        resolved.component = viewConfig.component ?? null;
        resolved.container = viewConfig.container ?? null;
        resolved.options = viewConfig.options || {};

        // STEP 5: Defaults
        resolved.owner = op.owner || "system";

        return resolved;
      };

      // ============================================================
      // ORCHESTRATION LAYER - Main run() function
      // ============================================================
      coworker.run = async function (op) {
        const start = Date.now();

        // Validation
        if (!op?.operation) {
          return this._failEarly("operation is required", start);
        }

        // Resolve all fields via config
        const resolved = this._resolveAll(op);

        // Merge options: config defaults + user overrides
        const mergedOptions = { ...resolved.options, ...op.options };

        // Construct run document
        const run_doc = {
          // Frappe standard fields
          doctype: "Run",
          name: generateId("run"),
          creation: start,
          modified: start,
          operation_key: JSON.stringify(op),    //added operation_key
          modified_by: resolved.owner || "system",
          docstatus: 0,
          owner: resolved.owner || "system",

          //compatibility with univeral doctype like Adapter
          config: op.config || {}, // ADDED config
          functions: op.functions || {}, // ADDED functions

          // Operation definition
          operation: resolved.operation,
          operation_original: op.operation,
          source: op.source || null, // ADDED use this for mutations of original + input
          source_doctype: resolved.source_doctype,
          target: op.target || null, // ADDED use this instead target
          target_doctype: resolved.target_doctype,

          // UI/Rendering (explicit takes priority over resolved)
          view: "view" in op ? op.view : resolved.view,
          component: "component" in op ? op.component : resolved.component,
          container: "container" in op ? op.container : resolved.container,

          // DATA - Delta architecture
          query: op.query || {},
          input: op.input || {},
          target: null,

          // Execution state
          state: {}, //ADDED state
          status: "running",
          success: false,
          error: null,
          duration: 0,

          // Hierarchy
          parent_run_id: mergedOptions.parentRunId || null,
          child_run_ids: [],

          // Flow context
          flow_id: op.flow_id || null,
          flow_template: op.flow_template || null,
          step_id: op.step_id || null,
          step_title: op.step_title || null,

          // Authorization
          agent: op.agent || null,

          // Options
          options: mergedOptions,

          // Runtime helpers
          child: null,
        };

        // Initialize draft mode
        if (run_doc.options.draft) {
          run_doc.input = run_doc.input || {};

          // For takeone with query, preserve the name for updates
          if (run_doc.query.where?.name && !run_doc.input.name) {
            run_doc.input.name = run_doc.query.where.name;
          }
        }

        // Define run.doc getter (computed merge of original + delta)
        Object.defineProperty(run_doc, "doc", {
          get() {
            const original = this.target?.data?.[0] || {};
            const delta = this.input || {};
            return this.options.draft ? { ...original, ...delta } : original;
          },
        });

        // Update state: RUNNING
        if (
          typeof CW !== "undefined" &&
          CW._updateFromRun
        ) {
          CW._updateFromRun(run_doc);
        }

        // ✅ IMPROVED: Child factory with context inheritance & tracking https://claude.ai/chat/c50f00d4-2043-404b-ad94-6e6d204da92e
        run_doc.child = async (cfg) => {
          const childRun = await coworker.run({
            // Spread user config first
            ...cfg,

            // ✅ Inherit parent context (unless explicitly overridden)
            flow_id: cfg.flow_id ?? run_doc.flow_id,
            flow_template: cfg.flow_template ?? run_doc.flow_template,
            agent: cfg.agent ?? run_doc.agent,

            // Merge options with parent context
            options: {
              // Parent context defaults
              adapter: run_doc.options?.adapter,

              // User overrides
              ...cfg.options,

              // ✅ Always set parentRunId
              parentRunId: run_doc.name,
            },
          });

          // ✅ Track bidirectional relationship
          if (!run_doc.child_run_ids.includes(childRun.name)) {
            run_doc.child_run_ids.push(childRun.name);

            // Update state if tracking is active
            if (
              typeof CW !== "undefined" &&
              CW._updateFromRun
            ) {
              CW._updateFromRun(run_doc);
            }
          }

          return childRun;
        };

        // Execute operation
        try {
          const result = await this._exec(run_doc);

          run_doc.target = result.target || result;
          run_doc.success = result.success === true;
          run_doc.error = result.error || null;

          // Copy doctype to input if missing (for saves)
          if (run_doc.options.draft && run_doc.target?.data?.[0]?.doctype) {
            if (!run_doc.input.doctype) {
              run_doc.input.doctype = run_doc.target.data[0].doctype;
            }
          }

          // Update state: COMPLETED
          run_doc.status = "completed";
          run_doc.duration = Date.now() - start;
          run_doc.modified = Date.now();

          if (
            typeof CW !== "undefined" &&
            CW._updateFromRun
          ) {
            CW._updateFromRun(run_doc);
          }
        } catch (err) {
          run_doc.success = false;
          run_doc.status = "failed";
          run_doc.error = {
            message: err.message,
            code:
              err.code ||
              `${run_doc.operation?.toUpperCase() || "OPERATION"}_FAILED`,
            stack:
              this.getConfig && this.getConfig("debug") ? err.stack : undefined,
          };

          // Update state: FAILED
          run_doc.duration = Date.now() - start;
          run_doc.modified = Date.now();

          if (
            typeof CW !== "undefined" &&
            CW._updateFromRun
          ) {
            CW._updateFromRun(run_doc);
          }
        }

        // Rendering (if system available)
        if (typeof this._render === "function") {
          this._render(run_doc);
        }

        return run_doc;
      };

      // ============================================================
      // EXECUTION ROUTER - Route through controller
      // ============================================================
      coworker._exec = async function (run_doc) {
        const previousAdapter = pb._currentAdapter;
        if (run_doc.options?.adapter) {
          pb.useAdapter(run_doc.options.adapter);
        }

        try {
          // ✅ A1: Route through controller (all operations)
          return await this.controller.execute(run_doc);
        } finally {
          pb.useAdapter(previousAdapter);
        }
      };

      // ============================================================
      // HELPER: EARLY FAILURE
      // ============================================================
      coworker._failEarly = function (message, start) {
        return {
          doctype: "Run",
          name: generateId("run"),
          creation: start,
          status: "failed",
          success: false,
          error: {
            message,
            code: "VALIDATION_FAILED",
          },
          duration: Date.now() - start,
        };
      };

      // ============================================================
      // CRUD HANDLERS (select, create, update, delete)
      // ✅ B2: All use coworker.* instead of this.*
      // ============================================================
      coworker._handlers = {
        // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
        // SELECT - Read operations
        // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
        select: async function (run_doc) {
          const { source_doctype, query, options } = run_doc;
          const { where, orderBy, take, skip, select } = query || {};
          const view = run_doc.view || query.view || "list";  //working const view = query.view || "list";
          const { includeSchema = true, includeMeta = false } = options || {};

          // Fetch schema if needed
          let schema = null;
          if (
            includeSchema &&
            source_doctype !== "All" &&
            //deleted source_doctype !== "Schema" &&
            source_doctype
          ) {
            //console.log("📥 Calling getSchema for:", source_doctype);
            schema = await coworker.getSchema(source_doctype);
            //console.log("📤 getSchema returned:", schema);
          } else {
            /*console.log("❌ Skipping schema fetch because:", {
              includeSchema,
              source_doctype,
              checks: {
                notAll: source_doctype !== "All",
                notSchema: source_doctype !== "Schema",
                exists: !!source_doctype,
              },
            });*/
          }

          // ✅ B2: Use coworker._buildPrismaWhere
          const queryDoctype = source_doctype === "All" ? "" : source_doctype;
          const pbFilter = coworker._buildPrismaWhere(queryDoctype, where);
          const pbSort = coworker._buildPrismaOrderBy(orderBy);

          const params = {};
          if (pbFilter) params.filter = pbFilter;
          if (pbSort) params.sort = pbSort;

          // ✅ B2: Use coworker._dbQuery
          const { data, meta } = await coworker._dbQuery(params, take, skip);

          // Field filtering based on view
          let filteredData = data;
          const shouldFilter = view === "list" || view === "card";

          /*if (view === "all") {     //dont work
    filteredData = data;
  }

          else*/ if (schema && !select && shouldFilter) {   //was if not else if
            const viewProp = `in_${view}_view`;
            const viewFields = schema.fields
              .filter((f) => f[viewProp])
              .map((f) => f.fieldname);
            const fields = ["name", ...viewFields];

            filteredData = data.map((item) => {
              const filtered = {
                doctype: source_doctype, // ✅ Always set doctype from source_doctype
              };
              fields.forEach((field) => {
                if (item.hasOwnProperty(field)) {
                  filtered[field] = item[field];
                }
              });
              return filtered;
            });
          } else if (select && Array.isArray(select)) {
            filteredData = data.map((item) => {
              const filtered = {};
              select.forEach((field) => {
                if (item.hasOwnProperty(field)) {
                  filtered[field] = item[field];
                }
              });
              return filtered;
            });
          }

          return {
            success: true,
            target: {
              data: filteredData,
              schema: includeSchema ? schema : undefined,
              meta: includeMeta ? meta : undefined,
              viewConfig: { layout: view === "card" ? "grid" : "table", view },
            },
          };
        },

        // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
        // TAKEONE - Single record (enforces take: 1)
        // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
        takeone: async function (run_doc) {
          if (!run_doc.query) run_doc.query = {};
          run_doc.query.take = 1;
          run_doc.query.view = "form";



          // ✅ B2: Use coworker._handlers.select (not this._handlers)
          const result = await coworker._handlers.select(run_doc);

          if (result.success && result.target?.data?.length > 1) {
            console.warn(
              `takeone returned ${result.target.data.length} records, using first only`,
            );
          }

          if (result.success && result.target?.data?.length === 0) {
            return {
              success: false,
              error: {
                message: "Record not found",
                code: "NOT_FOUND",
              },
            };
          }

          return result;
        },

        // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
        // CREATE - Insert operations (CORRECTED)
        // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
        // ✅ Updated (flexible)
        create: async function (run_doc) {
          const { target_doctype, input, options } = run_doc;
          const {
            includeSchema = true,
            includeMeta = false,
            applyRBAC = true,
          } = options || {};

          const inputData = input?.data || input;

          if (!inputData || Object.keys(inputData).length === 0) {
            throw new Error("CREATE requires input with data");
          }

          console.log("📝 CREATE handler:", {
            doctype: target_doctype,
            hasWrappedData: !!input?.data,
            fields: Object.keys(inputData),
          });

          // ✅ AUTO-APPLY RBAC PERMISSIONS (if not already present)
          let recordData = { ...inputData, doctype: target_doctype };

          if (applyRBAC && !inputData._allowed && !inputData._allowed_read) {
            console.log("🔐 Auto-applying RBAC for:", target_doctype);
            try {
              const perms = await coworker.rbac.applyPermissions(
                target_doctype,
                recordData,
              );
              recordData = perms;
              console.log("✅ RBAC applied:", {
                _allowed: recordData._allowed,
                _allowed_read: recordData._allowed_read,
              });
            } catch (error) {
              console.warn(
                "⚠️ RBAC failed, proceeding without:",
                error.message,
              );
              // Continue without RBAC if it fails
            }
          }

          // ✅ Fetch schema if needed
          let schema = null;
          if (includeSchema) {
            schema = await coworker.getSchema(target_doctype);
          }

          // ✅ Use proper abstraction layer (goes through adapter switch)
          const result = await coworker._dbCreate(recordData);

          console.log("✅ CREATE success:", result.data.name);

          return {
            success: true,
            target: {
              data: [result.data],
              schema: includeSchema ? schema : undefined,
              meta: includeMeta
                ? {
                    operation: "create",
                    created: 1,
                    id: result.meta?.id,
                    name: result.data.name,
                  }
                : undefined,
            },
          };
        },

        // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
        // HANDLER - Just Execution (No Logic) https://claude.ai/chat/a92d380b-8725-40c1-98f2-2486fc9ba997
        // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
        update: async function (run_doc) {
  const { source_doctype, target_doctype, input, query, options } = run_doc;  // ← Add target_doctype here
  const inputData = input?.data || input;
  const where = query?.where || query;

  const { includeSchema = true, includeMeta = false } = options || {};
  const doctype = source_doctype || target_doctype;  // ← Now target_doctype is defined

  let schema = null;
  if (includeSchema) {
    schema = await coworker.getSchema(doctype);  // ← Use doctype (not source_doctype)
  }

  const queryDoctype = source_doctype === "All" ? "" : source_doctype;
  const pbFilter = coworker._buildPrismaWhere(queryDoctype, where);

  const items =
    run_doc._items ||
    (await coworker._dbQuery({ filter: pbFilter })).data;

  if (items.length === 0) {
    return {
      success: true,
      target: { data: [], schema, meta: { updated: 0 } },
    };
  }

  // ✅ Process each update through field system
  const updates = await Promise.all(
    items.map(async (item) => {
      const merged = { ...item, ...inputData, doctype };  // ← Use doctype

      // ✅ SERIALIZE: Create temporary run_doc for processing
      const tempRunDoc = {
        operation: "update",
        target_doctype: doctype,  // ← Use target_doctype (field handlers check this)
        input: { data: merged },
        target: { schema },
      };

      // Apply field handlers (serialization)
      await coworker._applyFieldTypeHandlers(tempRunDoc);

      // Use processed data
      const result = await coworker._dbUpdate(
        item.name || item.id,
        tempRunDoc.input.data,
      );

      // ✅ DESERIALIZE result
      return {
        ...result,
        data: await coworker.deserializeDocument(
          result.data,
          doctype,  // ← Use doctype
        ),
      };
    }),
  );

  return {
    success: true,
    target: {
      data: updates.map((u) => u.data),
      schema,
      meta: { operation: "update", updated: updates.length },
    },
  };
},

        // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
        // DELETE - Remove operations
        // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
        delete: async function (run_doc) {
          const { source_doctype, query, options } = run_doc;
          const { where } = query || {};
          const { includeMeta = false } = options || {};

          if (!where || Object.keys(where).length === 0) {
            throw new Error(
              "DELETE requires query.where to prevent accidental mass deletion",
            );
          }

          // ✅ B2: Use coworker._buildPrismaWhere
          const queryDoctype = source_doctype === "All" ? "" : source_doctype;
          const pbFilter = coworker._buildPrismaWhere(queryDoctype, where);

          // Use pre-fetched items if controller provided them
          const items =
            run_doc._items ||
            (await coworker._dbQuery({ filter: pbFilter })).data;

          if (items.length === 0) {
            return {
              success: true,
              target: {
                data: [],
                meta: includeMeta
                  ? { operation: "delete", deleted: 0 }
                  : undefined,
              },
            };
          }

          // ✅ B2: Use coworker._dbDelete
          await Promise.all(items.map((item) => coworker._dbDelete(item.name)));

          return {
            success: true,
            target: {
              data: [],
              meta: includeMeta
                ? { operation: "delete", deleted: items.length }
                : undefined,
            },
          };
        },
      };

      // ============================================================
      // QUERY BUILDERS
      // ============================================================

      coworker._buildPrismaWhere = function (doctype, where) {
        const parts = [];

        if (doctype) {
          parts.push(`doctype = "${doctype}"`);
        }

        if (where) {
          const whereParts = this._buildWhereClause(where);
          if (whereParts) {
            parts.push(`(${whereParts})`);
          }
        }

        return parts.length > 0 ? parts.join(" && ") : undefined;
      };

      coworker._buildWhereClause = function (where) {
        if (!where || typeof where !== "object") return "";

        const parts = [];

        for (const [key, value] of Object.entries(where)) {
          // Logical operators
          if (key === "OR") {
            if (!Array.isArray(value) || value.length === 0) continue;
            const orParts = value
              .map((condition) => this._buildWhereClause(condition))
              .filter(Boolean);
            if (orParts.length > 0) {
              parts.push(`(${orParts.join(" || ")})`);
            }
            continue;
          }

          if (key === "AND") {
            if (!Array.isArray(value) || value.length === 0) continue;
            const andParts = value
              .map((condition) => this._buildWhereClause(condition))
              .filter(Boolean);
            if (andParts.length > 0) {
              parts.push(`(${andParts.join(" && ")})`);
            }
            continue;
          }

          if (key === "NOT") {
            const notClause = this._buildWhereClause(value);
            if (notClause) {
              parts.push(`!(${notClause})`);
            }
            continue;
          }

          // Regular field
          const fieldPath = this._getFieldPath(key);

          // Simple equality
          if (value === null || value === undefined) {
            parts.push(`${fieldPath} = null`);
            continue;
          }

          if (typeof value === "string") {
            parts.push(`${fieldPath} = "${value}"`);
            continue;
          }

          if (typeof value === "number" || typeof value === "boolean") {
            parts.push(`${fieldPath} = ${value}`);
            continue;
          }

          // Operators
          if (typeof value === "object" && !Array.isArray(value)) {
            for (const [op, opValue] of Object.entries(value)) {
              switch (op) {
                case "equals":
                  parts.push(
                    typeof opValue === "string"
                      ? `${fieldPath} = "${opValue}"`
                      : `${fieldPath} = ${opValue}`,
                  );
                  break;
                case "contains":
                  parts.push(`${fieldPath} ~ "${opValue}"`);
                  break;
                case "startsWith":
                  parts.push(`${fieldPath} ~ "^${opValue}"`);
                  break;
                case "endsWith":
                  parts.push(`${fieldPath} ~ "${opValue}$"`);
                  break;
                case "gt":
                  parts.push(`${fieldPath} > ${opValue}`);
                  break;
                case "gte":
                  parts.push(`${fieldPath} >= ${opValue}`);
                  break;
                case "lt":
                  parts.push(`${fieldPath} < ${opValue}`);
                  break;
                case "lte":
                  parts.push(`${fieldPath} <= ${opValue}`);
                  break;
                case "in":
                  if (Array.isArray(opValue) && opValue.length > 0) {
                    const inValues = opValue.map((v) =>
                      typeof v === "string"
                        ? `${fieldPath} = "${v}"`
                        : `${fieldPath} = ${v}`,
                    );
                    parts.push(`(${inValues.join(" || ")})`);
                  }
                  break;
                case "notIn":
                  if (Array.isArray(opValue) && opValue.length > 0) {
                    const notInValues = opValue.map((v) =>
                      typeof v === "string"
                        ? `${fieldPath} != "${v}"`
                        : `${fieldPath} != ${v}`,
                    );
                    parts.push(`(${notInValues.join(" && ")})`);
                  }
                  break;
                case "not":
                  if (opValue === null) {
                    parts.push(`${fieldPath} != null`);
                  } else if (typeof opValue === "string") {
                    parts.push(`${fieldPath} != "${opValue}"`);
                  } else if (typeof opValue === "object") {
                    const notClause = this._buildWhereClause({
                      [key]: opValue,
                    });
                    if (notClause) parts.push(`!(${notClause})`);
                  } else {
                    parts.push(`${fieldPath} != ${opValue}`);
                  }
                  break;
              }
            }
          }
        }

        return parts.join(" && ");
      };

      coworker._buildPrismaOrderBy = function (orderBy) {
        if (!orderBy) return undefined;

        if (Array.isArray(orderBy)) {
          return orderBy
            .map((obj) => {
              const [field, order] = Object.entries(obj)[0];
              const fieldPath = this._getFieldPath(field);
              return order === "desc" ? `-${fieldPath}` : `+${fieldPath}`;
            })
            .join(",");
        }

        return Object.entries(orderBy)
          .map(([field, order]) => {
            const fieldPath = this._getFieldPath(field);
            return order === "desc" ? `-${fieldPath}` : `+${fieldPath}`;
          })
          .join(",");
      };

      coworker._getFieldPath = function (fieldName) {
        if (["doctype", "name", "id"].includes(fieldName)) {
          return fieldName;
        }
        return `data.${fieldName}`;
      };

      // ============================================================
      // UTILITIES
      // ============================================================

      coworker._generateName = function (doctype) {
        const timestamp = Date.now();
        const random = Math.random().toString(36).substring(2, 8);
        return `${doctype.toLowerCase()}-${timestamp}-${random}`;
      };
3:35 PM

Now I have a clear picture. Let me map out what the SQLite adapter needs to implement:

The adapter must expose these db-level methods that the handlers call:

  • coworker._dbQuery(params, take, skip) — used by select/update/delete
  • coworker._dbCreate(recordData) — used by create
  • coworker._dbUpdate(id, data) — used by update
  • coworker._dbDelete(id) — used by delete

Filter format coming in is already the PocketBase string syntax like doctype = "Item" && (status = "active") — built by _buildPrismaWhere. So the SQLite adapter needs to parse that string into SQL WHERE clauses.

One question before I write it:

The data structure config you mentioned — you said you'd send it separately. I need that before building the table schema and the serialization/deserialization layer. Specifically:

  • What fields does your table have? Is data stored as a JSON blob in a data column, or as flat columns?
  • Looking at the code I see fields like name, doctype, data.* — suggests a structure like { name, doctype, data: {...} }. Is data stored as a JSON string in SQLite?

Send the data structure config and I'll build the full adapter.

no. lets use this time more universal approach _db(run_doc)

3:37 PM

Smart — one universal _db(run_doc) instead of 4 separate methods. The operation is already in run_doc.operation, so the adapter just switches on that.

So the interface becomes:

javascript
// Instead of:
coworker._dbQuery(params, take, skip)
coworker._dbCreate(recordData)
coworker._dbUpdate(id, data)
coworker._dbDelete(id)

// Single:
coworker._db(run_doc)  // reads operation, query, input from run_doc

A few quick confirms before I write it:

1. Data structure — still need this. Is it flat columns or a data JSON blob? I see data.* field paths in your filter builder which suggests JSON blob, but I want to confirm.

2. Registration — how do adapters get registered and switched? I see pb.useAdapter() and pb._currentAdapter in your run code. Should the SQLite adapter register itself the same way as the PocketBase one?

3. Table auto-creation — since config has the data structure, should _db create the table on first use if it doesn't exist?

Send the data structure config and answer those two, and I'll build it.

  1. Registration — how do adapters get registered and switched? I see pb.useAdapter() and pb._currentAdapter in your run code. Should the SQLite adapter register itself the same way as the PocketBase one?  I dont use this time this method. we will use run(operation, ..., query{}, options: {CW.Adapter.sqlite}) if options, we use this adapter
3:44 PM

Got it — so adapter selection is via options.adapter on the run call, and _db(run_doc) reads run_doc.options.adapter to know which adapter to use.

Still need the data structure config before I can write it. Send that and I'll build the full thing.

CREATE TABLE item (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,              -- reserved, DO not use in code
  name TEXT,                        -- used as id in code, unique
  doctype TEXT,
  docstatus INTEGER,                  -- docstatus 
  data TEXT DEFAULT '{}',            -- JSON data field
  owner TEXT DEFAULT '[]',           -- JSON array of user IDs
  _allowed TEXT DEFAULT '[]',        -- JSON array of role/user IDs (write)
  _allowed_read TEXT DEFAULT '[]',   -- JSON array of role/user IDs (read)
  created TEXT DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  updated TEXT DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
3:49 PM

Perfect. Now I have everything. Let me build it.

Done

Here's what was built and key design decisions:

Structure:

  • Single execute(run_doc) entry point — routes by run_doc.operation
  • coworker._db(run_doc) wired at the bottom, reads run_doc.options.adapter

Data mapping matches your schema exactly:

  • name, doctype, docstatus, owner, _allowed, _allowed_read → top-level columns
  • Everything else → data JSON blob, accessed via json_extract(data, '$.field')

Filter translation — PocketBase string doctype = "Item" && status = "active" becomes doctype = ? AND json_extract(data, '$.status') = ? with params array. The ~ operator maps to SQL LIKE.

Usage:

javascript
coworker.run({
  operation: "select",
  source_doctype: "Item",
  query: { where: { status: "active" }, take: 20, skip: 0 },
  options: { adapter: "sqlite" }
})

One thing to watch: your index.js server currently spreads params with ...params which works for single statements but your filter builder produces parameterized queries — make sure the server handles the params array correctly per statement. You may want to pass params per-statement rather than shared across a multi-statement query.

Pb adapter sqlite
JS 

dont change code. just advice. my data is in record.data all other top level fields excuding id is for fast access and querying. Advice if can eliminate without loosing functionality and speed and replace them with idexes derived from data.doctype etc

3:55 PM

Good question. Here's the honest analysis:

You can eliminate most of them, but not all without a tradeoff.

doctype and name are worth keeping as real columns. doctype is your primary filter in almost every query — without it as a column, every select would need json_extract(data, '$.doctype') which SQLite can index but it's messier and slightly slower. name is your primary key proxy, used in every update/delete by WHERE name = ? — keeping it a column is essentially free and very fast.

docstatus is borderline. If you filter by it often (e.g. docstatus = 1 for submitted records), keep it. If rarely, move it into data and add a functional index: CREATE INDEX idx_docstatus ON item(json_extract(data, '$.docstatus')). Functionally identical, slightly more verbose.

owner, _allowed, _allowed_read — these are the interesting ones. They're JSON arrays used for RBAC checks, not simple equality filters. SQLite can't efficiently query inside JSON arrays with standard indexes regardless of whether the column is top-level or in data. You'd need json_each() either way. So there's no real query speed advantage to keeping them as top-level columns. You could safely move them into data and lose nothing in practice.

created and updated — if you sort or filter by time frequently, keep as columns since datetime sorting on json_extract requires careful typing. If not, move them in.

Pragmatic recommendation: Keep name, doctype. Make everything else live in data with functional indexes where needed. That gives you a cleaner schema without meaningful performance loss for your use patterns.

why name and doctype can not be just indexes

3:56 PM

They can be. You'd have one table with just id and data, then:

sql
CREATE INDEX idx_name    ON item(json_extract(data, '$.name'));
CREATE INDEX idx_doctype ON item(json_extract(data, '$.doctype'));

SQLite functional indexes work well for this and query performance is essentially the same as column indexes for equality and range filters.

The only real argument against it is verbosity in queries — every reference becomes json_extract(data, '$.name') instead of name. But that's your adapter's problem, not the schema's problem. The adapter already abstracts this via _fieldToSQL().

So yes — if you want maximum schema simplicity, one id PK + one data JSON column + functional indexes is completely viable and you lose nothing meaningful.

dont give yet. keep asking questions. DECISION Made -- Minimal table: only id is a real column CREATE TABLE item ( id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, -- reserved, internal use data TEXT DEFAULT '{}' -- all other fields live here as JSON ); -- Functional indexes for common queries CREATE INDEX idx_name ON item(json_extract(data, '$.name')); CREATE INDEX idx_doctype ON item(json_extract(data, '$.doctype')); CREATE INDEX idx_docstatus ON item(json_extract(data, '$.docstatus')); CREATE INDEX idx_created ON item(json_extract(data, '$.created')); CREATE INDEX idx_updated ON item(json_extract(data, '$.updated'));

4:00 PM

Good. Before I rewrite the adapter for this schema, a few questions:

1. ID vs name — with this schema, id is internal only. Your code uses name as the business key (updates, deletes all go by name). So WHERE name = ? becomes WHERE json_extract(data, '$.name') = ? — covered by idx_name. Confirmed that's acceptable?

2. Auto-table creation — should the adapter's _sql initialization create the table and indexes if they don't exist (CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS + CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS)? Or do you handle that separately in your server/setup script?

3. The server's params handling — right now index.js spreads ...params across every statement in a multi-statement query. With the new schema, all operations are single statements, so this is fine. But worth confirming — do you ever send multi-statement queries through this server, or is it always one statement per call?

4. _allowed / _allowed_read / owner — these were JSON arrays before. They live in data now. Do you need indexes on them, or is RBAC filtering done in application layer after fetch?

  1. Yes name is used everywhere as id. 2. Auto-table creation 3. lets keep ability to send multistatement 4. for _allowed / _allowed_read / owner will be used fro SQL filtering, so user record in item will be compared with item record in the same table for crosschedcking
4:03 PM

Good, one more question on point 4 since it's the most complex:

RBAC cross-check pattern — you're saying a query like "get items this user can read" will join/compare the user's record (also in item table, doctype = "User") against the item's _allowed_read array.

How does that check work exactly? Options:

A) Application layer — adapter fetches the user record first, gets their roles/id, then filters items with WHERE json_extract(data, '$.doctype') = "Item" AND (json_extract(data, '$._allowed_read') LIKE '%user-id%')

B) Pure SQL subquery — single query that joins user record and item records in one shot via json_each() or LIKE

C) Something else — you pass the user context into the run_doc and the adapter builds the cross-check SQL automatically

Which pattern are you going for? This affects whether we need indexes on _allowed/_allowed_read, and whether the adapter needs to know about the user context or just execute what it's given.

Pure SQL subquery we have run(select, doctype=Task, query {where status = Open}} BUT not all records are allowed for users so its rather complex

4:05 PM

Right, so the full query needs to be something like:

sql
SELECT i.* FROM item i
WHERE json_extract(i.data, '$.doctype') = 'Task'
AND json_extract(i.data, '$.status') = 'Open'
AND EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM json_each(json_extract(i.data, '$._allowed_read')) ae
  WHERE ae.value = ?  -- current user's name/id
)

But a user probably has roles, not just an id — so _allowed_read likely contains a mix of user names and role names. That means the subquery needs to check if any of the user's roles OR their direct id appears in _allowed_read.

Before I finalize this pattern, I need to know:

What does _allowed_read actually contain? For example:

  • ["user-123"] — just user ids?
  • ["role-admin", "role-editor"] — just role names?
  • ["user-123", "role-admin"] — mixed?

And where is the current user's identity/roles coming from at query time — is it passed in run_doc.options (like options.user, options.roles), or does the adapter need to fetch the user record from the same item table first?

they are just json capabilities roles, ids in one set we cross-check. BUT THIS SHOULD BE SQL filter 

coworker.rbac = {
  
  // ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  // EXTRACT SCHEMA PERMISSIONS
  // ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  async getDocTypePermissions(doctype) {
    // Use coworker.getSchema() instead of direct DB call
    const schema = await coworker.getSchema(doctype);
    
    if (!schema) {
      throw new Error(`Schema not found for doctype: ${doctype}`);
    }
    
    const permissions = schema.permissions || [];
    
    const writeRoles = [];
    const readOnlyRoles = [];
    
    for (const perm of permissions) {
      if (perm.write) {
        writeRoles.push(perm.role);
      } else if (perm.read) {
        readOnlyRoles.push(perm.role);
      }
    }
    
    return {
      _allowed: writeRoles.map(role => generateId("Role", role)),
      _allowed_read: readOnlyRoles.map(role => generateId("Role", role)),
      roleNames: {
        write: writeRoles,
        read: readOnlyRoles
      }
    };
  },

  // ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  // ENSURE ROLES EXIST (AUTO-CREATE MISSING)
  // ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  async ensureRolesExist(doctype) {
    const doctypePerms = await this.getDocTypePermissions(doctype);
    const roleSchemaPerms = await this.getDocTypePermissions("Role");
    
    const allRoleNames = [
      ...doctypePerms.roleNames.write,
      ...doctypePerms.roleNames.read
    ];
    
    for (const roleName of allRoleNames) {
      const roleId = generateId("Role", roleName);
      
      // Use run('select') instead of direct DB call
      const checkRun = await coworker.run({
        operation: 'select',
        source_doctype: 'Role',
        query: {
          where: { id: roleId },
          take: 1
        },
        options: { 
          render: false,
          includeSchema: false
        }
      });
      
      if (checkRun.success && checkRun.target?.data?.length > 0) {
        console.log(`✓ Role exists: ${roleName}`);
      } else {
        console.log(`Creating role: ${roleName}`);
        
        // Use run('create') instead of direct DB call
        const createRun = await coworker.run({
          operation: 'create',
          target_doctype: 'Role',
          input: {
            id: roleId,
            name: roleName,
            doctype: 'Role',
            docstatus: 0,
            owner: '',
            ...roleSchemaPerms,
            role_name: roleName
          },
          options: {
            render: false,
            includeSchema: false
          }
        });
        
        if (createRun.success) {
          console.log(`✓ Created role: ${roleName}`);
        } else {
          console.error(`Failed to create role: ${roleName}`, createRun.error);
        }
      }
    }
    
    return {
      _allowed: doctypePerms._allowed,
      _allowed_read: doctypePerms._allowed_read
    };
  },

  // ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

  //Prototype of function
  // CHECK USER PERMISSION
  // ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  async checkPermission(userId, record, operation = "read") {
    // Get user's roles
    const userRun = await coworker.run({     //wrong - we resolve from jwt
      operation: 'select',
      source_doctype: 'User',
      query: {
        where: { id: userId },
        take: 1
      },
      options: { 
        render: false,
        includeSchema: false
      }
    });
    
    if (!userRun.success || !userRun.target?.data?.length) {
      return false;
    }
    
    const user = userRun.target.data[0];
    const userRoles = user._allowed_read || []; // User's capabilities
    
    // Check against record's ACL
    if (operation === "write" || operation === "update" || operation === "delete") {
      const allowed = record._allowed || [];
      return userRoles.some(role => allowed.includes(role));
    }
    
    if (operation === "read") {
      const allowed = record._allowed || [];
      const allowedRead = record._allowed_read || [];
      return userRoles.some(role => 
        allowed.includes(role) || allowedRead.includes(role)
      );
    }
    
    return false;
  },

  // ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  // APPLY PERMISSIONS TO CREATE/UPDATE
  // ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  async applyPermissions(doctype, data) {
    const perms = await this.ensureRolesExist(doctype);
    
    return {
      ...data,
      ...perms
    };
  }
};
4:07 PM

Now I see the full picture. The RBAC check pattern is clear:

  • User record has _allowed_read = array of role/capability ids they possess
  • Item record has _allowed_read = array of role ids that can read it
  • Cross-check = does user's capability set intersect with item's allowed set

So the SQL filter needs to be:

sql
-- get user's capabilities first (from JWT or user record)
-- then filter items where ANY of user's roles appear in item's _allowed_read

WHERE EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM json_each(json_extract(i.data, '$._allowed_read')) item_role
  WHERE item_role.value IN (
    SELECT user_role.value FROM json_each(json_extract(u.data, '$._allowed_read')) user_role
    WHERE json_extract(u.data, '$.name') = ?  -- current user name
  )
)

But I see the comment in your code: //wrong - we resolve from jwt. So:

Key question before I finalize: Does the adapter receive the current user's roles/capabilities already resolved (passed in run_doc.options.user or run_doc.options.roles), or does it need to fetch the user record from the item table itself as a subquery?

This is the last thing I need — it determines whether RBAC is one SQL query or two.

dont harry up. we are not final with RBAC (its just part). you dont fetch user run_doc.user._allowed_read is extracted by jwt resolver and set in run_doc But lets check the understanding of RBAC. explain this 

. Access Control Rules
ViewRule (Read Access):
javascriptowner = @request.auth.id ||
_allowed_read:each ?~ 'roleispublic' ||
@request.auth.item_via_user_id._allowed_read:each ?= _allowed:each ||
@request.auth.item_via_user_id._allowed_read:each ?= _allowed_read:each
UpdateRule (Write Access):
javascriptowner = @request.auth.id ||
@request.auth.item_via_user_id._allowed_read:each ?= _allowed:each
4:15 PM

This is PocketBase's native rule syntax. Let me decode it:

ViewRule (Read Access) — a user can see a record if ANY of these is true:

  1. owner = @request.auth.id — the authenticated user IS the owner of the record
  2. _allowed_read:each ?~ 'roleispublic' — any value in the record's _allowed_read array contains the string 'roleispublic' — meaning the record is publicly readable
  3. @request.auth.item_via_user_id._allowed_read:each ?= _allowed:each — traverse from the auth user → their User record in item table (via item_via_user_id relation) → get their _allowed_read capabilities → check if ANY of those intersects with the record's _allowed (write roles). Meaning: if you have write-level capability, you can also read.
  4. @request.auth.item_via_user_id._allowed_read:each ?= _allowed_read:each — same traversal but intersects with record's _allowed_read. Meaning: if you have a matching read capability, you can read.

UpdateRule (Write Access) — stricter, only 2 conditions:

  1. owner = @request.auth.id — you own it
  2. @request.auth.item_via_user_id._allowed_read:each ?= _allowed:each — your capabilities intersect with the record's write-allowed set

The core pattern: User capabilities (_allowed_read on User record) are matched against record permissions (_allowed for write, _allowed_read for read). A user with write capability implicitly has read access too.

Is this the logic you want to replicate in pure SQL?

Yes.

4:16 PM

Good. So the full RBAC logic translated to SQL conditions for a SELECT:

sql
-- User can read a record if ANY of these:

-- 1. They own it
json_extract(i.data, '$.owner') = :user_name

-- 2. Record is public
EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM json_each(json_extract(i.data, '$._allowed_read')) r
  WHERE r.value = 'roleispublic'
)

-- 3. User's capabilities intersect with record's _allowed (write = can also read)
EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM json_each(json_extract(i.data, '$._allowed')) rec_role
  JOIN json_each(:user_allowed_read) usr_role ON usr_role.value = rec_role.value
)

-- 4. User's capabilities intersect with record's _allowed_read
EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM json_each(json_extract(i.data, '$._allowed_read')) rec_role
  JOIN json_each(:user_allowed_read) usr_role ON usr_role.value = rec_role.value
)

Where :user_name and :user_allowed_read come directly from run_doc.user (already resolved from JWT).

Is my understanding correct before we continue?

Also one structural question — should this RBAC SQL filter be always applied by the adapter automatically when run_doc.user is present, or should it be opt-in via something like options.rbac = true?

lets check 


**Rule Evaluation Flow:**
```
Request arrives
    ↓
Validate JWT → Extract userId
    ↓
Fetch User profile from DB (WHERE id = userId AND doctype = "User")
    ↓
Load user's _allowed_read (capabilities) into @request.auth.item_via_user_id
    ↓
Fetch target record from DB
    ↓
Evaluate ViewRule/UpdateRule
    ↓
GRANT or DENY

Security Properties
Property 1: Fail-Closed by Default
No permissions = No access
javascript// Record with no permissions
{
  owner: "",
  _allowed: [],
  _allowed_read: []
}
Result: Only System Manager can access (via separate admin privileges), regular users are denied.
Security Benefit:

✅ Forgotten permissions don't leak data
✅ Bugs that clear arrays block access (safe failure mode)
✅ Explicit opt-in required for any access


Property 2: Defense in Depth (Layered Checks)
Multiple ways to grant access, evaluated in order:

Ownership (owner = @request.auth.id)

Fastest check
Bypasses role checks
User directly owns the record


Public access (_allowed_read:each ?~ 'roleispublic')

System-level marker
No user capability check needed
Enables unauthenticated access


Write role intersection (@request.auth.item_via_user_id._allowed_read:each ?= _allowed:each)

User's capabilities ∩ Record's write permissions
If user has ANY required role → GRANT


Read role intersection (@request.auth.item_via_user_id._allowed_read:each ?= _allowed_read:each)

User's capabilities ∩ Record's read permissions
Read-only access path



Security Benefit:

✅ Multiple legitimate access paths
✅ Each layer independently secure
✅ Compromise of one layer doesn't break others


Property 3: Capability Immutability During Request
User capabilities are loaded ONCE at request start:
javascript// Request lifecycle
1. JWT validated → userId extracted
2. User profile fetched from DB → capabilities loaded
3. Capabilities frozen in @request.auth.item_via_user_id
4. All permission checks use this snapshot
5. Request completes
Security Benefit:

✅ No time-of-check-time-of-use (TOCTOU) attacks
✅ User cannot modify capabilities mid-request
✅ Atomic permission evaluation


Property 4: Separation of Privileges
Users cannot escalate their own privileges:
javascript// John's profile
{
  id: "userjohnxxxxx",
  owner: "",                          // ⚠️ Empty = John doesn't own his profile
  _allowed: [roleSystemManager],      // ⚠️ Only System Manager can edit
  _allowed_read: [roleProjectsUser]   // John's current capabilities
}

// John tries to edit his profile
UpdateRule check:
  owner = @request.auth.id?
    → "" ≠ "userjohnxxxxx" → FAIL
  
  @request.auth.item_via_user_id._allowed_read:each ?= _allowed:each?
    → [roleProjectsUser] ∩ [roleSystemManager] = ∅ → FAIL

RESULT: 403 Forbidden
Why this works:

John's profile has owner: "" (no ownership)
John's capabilities = [roleProjectsUser]
Profile requires roleSystemManager to edit
John doesn't have roleSystemManager in his capabilities
Catch-22: John can't add roleSystemManager to his capabilities because he doesn't already have it

Security Benefit:

✅ Users cannot grant themselves new roles
✅ Users cannot remove roles from themselves
✅ Users cannot edit their own permissions
✅ Only System Manager (or those with edit permission) can modify user capabilities


Property 5: Token Contains Identity Only, Not Capabilities
JWT structure:
javascript// ❌ INSECURE - Don't do this
{
  userId: "userjohnxxxxx",
  capabilities: [roleProjectsUser, roleManager]  // Attacker can forge
}

// ✅ SECURE - Minimal token
{
  userId: "userjohnxxxxx",
  iat: 1704067200,
  exp: 1704070800
}
Capabilities are ALWAYS fetched from database:
javascript// On every request
const userProfile = await db.query(
  'SELECT * FROM item WHERE id = ? AND doctype = "User"',
  [token.userId]
);

const capabilities = JSON.parse(userProfile._allowed_read);
// Use these capabilities, never trust token claims
Security Benefit:

✅ Token forgery cannot grant elevated access
✅ Capability changes take effect on next request (no stale permissions)
✅ Database is single source of truth
✅ Token is proof of identity, not authority
4:17 PM

This is a solid, well-thought-out security model. My understanding checks out. A few observations:

What you got right:

The fail-closed default is the most important property — empty permissions = no access. Most systems fail-open which leaks data on misconfiguration.

The capability immutability (Property 3) is critical for SQL implementation — since run_doc.user._allowed_read is loaded once from JWT→DB at request start and frozen, the adapter never needs to re-fetch the user. It just uses what's in run_doc.user.

Property 5 (token contains identity only) means the adapter's RBAC SQL always needs the DB fetch of user capabilities — you can't shortcut by reading roles from the token itself.

One thing to clarify in your model:

You show:

javascript
const capabilities = JSON.parse(userProfile._allowed_read);

But with the new minimal schema, _allowed_read lives inside data JSON, so it's actually:

javascript
const profile = JSON.parse(userProfile.data);
const capabilities = profile._allowed_read;

Just needs updating in your JWT resolver wherever it extracts capabilities from the fetched user record.

Overall: the model is consistent and the SQL I drafted earlier matches all 4 rule conditions correctly. Ready to continue whenever you want to move to the next piece.

in app js code (record = record.data)

4:18 PM

Good point. So in app code the developer always works with the deserialized record directly — record.name, record._allowed_read, record.status etc. The data wrapper is purely a storage detail handled by the adapter layer. The app never sees it.

That means the adapter's _deserializeRow is the only place that unwraps data, and _serializeRow (on write) is the only place that wraps it back. Everything above that layer is transparent.

Confirmed — ready for the next piece whenever you are.

yes the adapter should unpack data:

4:19 PM

Confirmed. The adapter contract is:

  • Read (output): _deserializeRow unpacks data JSON and merges it flat — app sees record.name, record.status, record._allowed_read directly
  • Write (input): _serializeRow takes the flat record and packs everything except reserved fields back into data JSON for storage

The data column is invisible to the app layer. Adapter handles it entirely.

Ready for next piece.

yes

4:19 PM

Ready. What's the next piece?

lets refactor adapter based on all above (like no more top level fields other then id, data etc

4:20 PM
Done

Key changes from the previous version:

Schema — id + data only. No more top-level columns.

_col(field) — single place that maps any field name to SQL. id → real column, everything else → json_extract(data, '$.field'). This means name, doctype, _allowed_read, status — all go through json_extract automatically.

_unpack(row) — just JSON.parse(row.data). The flat record the app sees has no wrapper.

_pack(record) — just JSON.stringify(record). Everything goes in.

RBAC — unchanged logic, but now queries json_extract(data, '$._allowed_read') etc. since there are no top-level columns anymore.

init() — creates table and all functional indexes. Call once on startup.

Pb adapter sqlite
JS 

dont change code, just question. why are u sure that your codeing with .this will work'

4:33 PM

I'm not sure. It's a valid concern.

The adapter is a plain object literal, not a class. So this inside methods like _buildWhere, _whereToSQL, _rbacFilter etc. depends entirely on how the method is called. If called as adapter._buildWhere(run_doc) — this is the adapter object, works fine. But if ever destructured or passed as a callback, this breaks silently.

Specific risk spots:

javascript
// These call each other via this — fragile
const where = this._buildWhere(run_doc);      // calls this._whereToSQL
const sort  = this._buildSort(orderBy);        // calls this._col

If any of these get detached from the object context, this._col or this._whereToSQL becomes undefined and fails.

Two safe alternatives:

Option A — replace all this with the explicit object reference:

javascript
pb._adapters.sqlite._col(field)  // always works regardless of call context

Option B — use a module pattern with closed-over functions instead of an object with this:

javascript
const _col   = (field) => ...
const _unpack = (row) => ...
// no this anywhere

Option B is cleaner and matches your existing memory adapter style which also avoids this in helpers. Which do you prefer?

u see, i am storing adapters in pre-compiled json. please give me in valid json without comments and syntax that prevents saving. I am serializing and deserializing json mysell. so funcitons: { "init": "function(run_doc) { const adapter = run_doc.target.data[0]; const config = adapter.config; globalThis.pb = globalThis.pb || new PocketBase(config.url); globalThis.pb.autoCancellation(config.autoCancellation); console.log('✓ PocketBase initialized:', config.url); return run_doc; }", "select": "async function(run_doc) { const adapter = run_doc.target.data[0]; const query = run_doc.query || {}; const collection = adapter.config.defaultCollection; const params = {}; if (query.filter) params.filter = query.filter; if (query.sort) params.sort = query.sort; const take = query.take; const skip = query.skip; let result; let items; let metaData; if (take !== undefined) { const page = skip ? Math.floor(skip / take) + 1 : 1; result = await globalThis.pb.collection(collection).getList(page, take, params); items = result.items; metaData = { total: result.totalItems, page: result.page, pageSize: result.perPage, totalPages: result.totalPages, hasMore: result.page < result.totalPages }; } else { items = await globalThis.pb.collection(collection).getFullList(params); metaData = { total: items.length, page: 1, pageSize: items.length, totalPages: 1, hasMore: false }; } run_doc.output = { data: items.map(item => item.data).filter(data => data != null), meta: metaData }; run_doc.success = true; return run_doc; }", "insert": "async function(run_doc) { const adapter = run_doc.target.data[0]; const data = run_doc.input?.data || run_doc.input; const collection = adapter.config.defaultCollection; const recordId = typeof generateId === 'function' ? generateId(data.doctype?.toLowerCase() || 'record') : new-${Date.now()}-${Math.random().toString(36).substr(2, 9)}; const doctype = data.doctype; if (!doctype) throw new Error('CREATE requires doctype'); const completeData = { id: recordId, name: recordId, doctype: doctype, ...data }; const created = await globalThis.pb.collection(collection).create({ id: recordId, name: recordId, doctype: doctype, data: completeData }); run_doc.output = { data: created.data, meta: { id: created.id, name: created.name, created: created.created, doctype: created.doctype } }; run_doc.success = true; return run_doc; }", "update": "async function(run_doc) { const adapter = run_doc.target.data[0]; const data = run_doc.input?.data || run_doc.input; const identifier = run_doc.input?.id || run_doc.input?.name; const collection = adapter.config.defaultCollection; if (!identifier) throw new Error('UPDATE requires id or name'); let recordId; let recordName; let existingRecord; const isPocketBaseId = /^[a-z0-9]{15}$/.test(identifier); if (isPocketBaseId) { recordId = identifier; recordName = identifier; existingRecord = await globalThis.pb.collection(collection).getOne(recordId); } else { const records = await globalThis.pb.collection(collection).getFullList({ filter: data.name = \"${identifier}\" }); if (records.length === 0) throw new Error(Record not found: ${identifier}); existingRecord = records[0]; recordId = existingRecord.id; recordName = existingRecord.name || existingRecord.id; } const doctype = data.doctype || existingRecord.doctype; if (!doctype) throw new Error('UPDATE requires doctype'); const completeData = { id: recordId, name: recordName, doctype: doctype, ...data }; const updated = await globalThis.pb.collection(collection).update(recordId, { name: recordName, doctype: doctype, data: completeData }); run_doc.output = { data: updated.data, meta: { id: updated.id, name: updated.name, updated: updated.updated, doctype: updated.doctype } }; run_doc.success = true; return run_doc; }", "delete": "async function(run_doc) { const adapter = run_doc.target.data[0]; const identifier = run_doc.input?.id || run_doc.input?.name; const collection = adapter.config.defaultCollection; if (!identifier) throw new Error('DELETE requires id or name'); let recordId; const isPocketBaseId = /^[a-z0-9]{15}$/.test(identifier); if (isPocketBaseId) { recordId = identifier; } else { const records = await globalThis.pb.collection(collection).getFullList({ filter: data.name = \"${identifier}\" }); if (records.length === 0) throw new Error(Record not found: ${identifier}); recordId = records[0].id; } await globalThis.pb.collection(collection).delete(recordId); run_doc.output = { success: true, meta: { id: recordId, deleted: true } }; run_doc.success = true; return run_doc; }" }

4:39 PM

Now I understand the full pattern. Functions are stored as strings in JSON, deserialized and eval'd at runtime. So this is completely irrelevant — each function receives run_doc and resolves everything it needs from there, including the adapter config via run_doc.target.data[0].

So the SQLite adapter functions need to follow the same pattern — no this, no object methods, everything self-contained in each function string, config and helpers accessed via closure or inline.

Before I write it, one question: where do shared helpers live? In the PocketBase adapter I see each function is fully self-contained — logic is inlined (like the PocketBase ID check regex). Should the SQLite adapter do the same — inline everything per function — or do you have a helpers or utils key in the adapter JSON where shared functions like _col, _whereToSQL, _rbacFilter can live and be accessed at runtime?

lets store them in adapter BUT mind how i compile adapters from outside 

compileDocument: async function (run_doc) {
  const docs = Array.isArray(run_doc.target?.data)
    ? run_doc.target.data
    : [run_doc.target?.data].filter(Boolean);

  for (const doc of docs) {
    if (!globalThis[doc.doctype]) globalThis[doc.doctype] = {};

    // Derive semantic alias from schema instead of hardcoding adapter_name
    const autoname = CW.Schema?.[doc.doctype]?.autoname;
    const semanticField = autoname?.startsWith('field:') ? autoname.slice(6) : null;
    const semanticValue = semanticField ? doc[semanticField] : null;

    // Load Scripts
    if (Array.isArray(doc.scripts)) {
      for (const script of doc.scripts) {
        if (script.type === 'sdk' || (!script.type && script.src)) {
          const scriptNs = script.namespace || semanticValue || doc.name;
          if (globalThis[scriptNs]) continue;
          if (script.source?.trim()) {
            (0, eval)(script.source);
          } else if (script.src) {
            const response = await fetch(script.src);
            script.source = await response.text();
            (0, eval)(script.source);
          }
        }
      }
    }

    // Compile Functions
    const runtime = { config: doc.config };
    if (doc.functions) {
      Object.entries(doc.functions).forEach(([name, fnStr]) => {
        runtime[name] = eval(`(${fnStr})`);
      });
    }
    if (runtime.init) runtime.init(run_doc);

    // Register under PK always, semantic alias when available
    globalThis[doc.doctype][doc.name] = runtime;
    if (semanticValue && semanticValue !== doc.name) {
      globalThis[doc.doctype][semanticValue] = runtime;
    }
  }

  this._invalidateIndex();
},
4:40 PM

Now I see it clearly. The compiler does this:

javascript
const runtime = { config: doc.config };
Object.entries(doc.functions).forEach(([name, fnStr]) => {
  runtime[name] = eval(`(${fnStr})`);
});

So runtime is the compiled object. All functions land on it. Which means helpers stored in functions are also compiled onto runtime and accessible via closure if functions reference each other — but only if they close over runtime explicitly, since there's no this.

So the pattern for shared helpers is:

json
{
  "functions": {
    "_col": "function(field) { ... }",
    "_whereToSQL": "function(where) { ... }",
    "select": "async function(run_doc) { const _col = runtime._col; ... }"
  }
}

But runtime won't be in scope inside the eval'd functions unless explicitly passed or closed over.

The question before I write: how do helper functions reference each other at runtime? Options:

A) Each function receives runtime via run_doc (e.g. run_doc.runtime)

B) Helpers are inlined into each function that needs them (fully self-contained, like PocketBase adapter)

C) Something else — the compiler puts runtime into a known global like globalThis.Adapter[name] so functions can reach it

Which is it?

// ============================================================
// globalThis.CW - Centralized State & Runtime. ADDED 
// ============================================================

globalThis.CW = {
  runs: {}, // indexed by run.name
  runsByOpKey: {}, // indexed by operation_key
  current_run: null,
  _index: null,

  // ─── Run Management ─────────────────────────────────────
  _updateFromRun: function(run_doc) {
    this.runs[run_doc.name] = run_doc;

    if (run_doc.operation_key) {
      this.runsByOpKey[run_doc.operation_key] = run_doc;
    }

    // Only track navigation runs as "current"
    if (
      run_doc.component?.startsWith("Main") &&
      run_doc.options?.render !== false
    ) {
      this.current_run = run_doc.name;
    }

    // Invalidate index when runs change
    this._invalidateIndex();

    globalThis.dispatchEvent(
      new CustomEvent("coworker:state:change", {
        detail: { run: run_doc },
      }),
