same story await CW.compileAll(); coworker-state.js:167 Uncaught SyntaxError: Unexpected token '-' at coworker-state.js:166:34

5:36 PM

Show me line 166-167 of coworker-state.js. The error is in your compiler code, not the adapter functions — Unexpected token '-' at that specific line means the issue is in how compileAll evals the string, not in the function strings themselves since we just confirmed those eval fine.

it works with all other 

// ============================================================
// globalThis.CW - Centralized State & Runtime. ADDED 
// ============================================================

globalThis.CW = {
  runs: {}, // indexed by run.name
  runsByOpKey: {}, // indexed by operation_key
  current_run: null,
  _index: null,

  // ─── Run Management ─────────────────────────────────────
  _updateFromRun: function(run_doc) {
    this.runs[run_doc.name] = run_doc;

    if (run_doc.operation_key) {
      this.runsByOpKey[run_doc.operation_key] = run_doc;
    }

    // Only track navigation runs as "current"
    if (
      run_doc.component?.startsWith("Main") &&
      run_doc.options?.render !== false
    ) {
      this.current_run = run_doc.name;
    }

    // Invalidate index when runs change
    this._invalidateIndex();

    globalThis.dispatchEvent(
      new CustomEvent("coworker:state:change", {
        detail: { run: run_doc },
      }),
    );
  },

  getRun: function(run_id) {
    return this.runs[run_id];
  },

  getCurrentRun: function() {
    return this.runs[this.current_run];
  },

  getAllRuns: function() {
    return Object.values(this.runs);
  },

  getRunsByStatus: function(status) {
    return Object.values(this.runs).filter((r) => r.status === status);
  },

  clear: function() {
    this.runs = {};
    this.current_run = null;
    this._invalidateIndex();
  },

  findByOperation: function(op) {
    const key = JSON.stringify(op);
    return this.runsByOpKey[key];
  },

  _removeRun: function(run_name) {
    const run = this.runs[run_name];
    if (run) {
      delete this.runs[run_name];
      if (run.operation_key) {
        delete this.runsByOpKey[run.operation_key];
      }
      this._invalidateIndex();
    }
  },

  // ─── Index Management ───────────────────────────────────
_buildIndex: function () {
  if (this._index) return;
  this._index = {};

  const allDocs = [];

  // Pass 1: index everything by name (PK) only
  for (const run of Object.values(this.runs)) {
    const docs = run.target?.data;
    if (!Array.isArray(docs)) continue;

    for (const doc of docs) {
      if (!doc?.doctype || !doc?.name) continue;
      if (!this._index[doc.doctype]) this._index[doc.doctype] = {};
      const runtime = globalThis[doc.doctype]?.[doc.name];
      this._index[doc.doctype][doc.name] = runtime || doc;
      allDocs.push(doc);
    }
  }

  // Pass 1.5: build doctype->autoname map directly from Schema index
  const autonameMap = {};
  for (const entry of Object.values(this._index['Schema'] || {})) {
    if (entry?.schema_name && entry?.autoname) {
      autonameMap[entry.schema_name] = entry.autoname;
    }
  }

  // Pass 2: semantic aliases using autonameMap
  for (const doc of allDocs) {
    const autoname = autonameMap[doc.doctype];
    if (autoname?.startsWith('field:')) {
      const semanticField = autoname.slice(6);
      const semanticValue = doc[semanticField];
      if (semanticValue && semanticValue !== doc.name) {
        this._index[doc.doctype][semanticValue] = this._index[doc.doctype][doc.name];
      }
    }
  }
},

  _invalidateIndex: function() {
    this._index = null;
  },

  getDoctype: function(doctype) {
    this._buildIndex();
    return this._index[doctype] || {};
  },

  getDocument: function(doctype, name) {
    this._buildIndex();
    return this._index[doctype]?.[name];
  },

  // ─── Compilation ────────────────────────────────────────
_compileDocument: async function (run_doc) {
  const docs = Array.isArray(run_doc.target?.data)
    ? run_doc.target.data
    : [run_doc.target?.data].filter(Boolean);

  for (const doc of docs) {
    if (!globalThis[doc.doctype]) globalThis[doc.doctype] = {};

    // Derive semantic alias from schema instead of hardcoding adapter_name
    const autoname = CW.Schema?.[doc.doctype]?.autoname;
    const semanticField = autoname?.startsWith('field:') ? autoname.slice(6) : null;
    const semanticValue = semanticField ? doc[semanticField] : null;

    // Load Scripts
    if (Array.isArray(doc.scripts)) {
      for (const script of doc.scripts) {
        if (script.type === 'sdk' || (!script.type && script.src)) {
          const scriptNs = script.namespace || semanticValue || doc.name;
          if (globalThis[scriptNs]) continue;
          if (script.source?.trim()) {
            (0, eval)(script.source);
          } else if (script.src) {
            const response = await fetch(script.src);
            script.source = await response.text();
            (0, eval)(script.source);
          }
        }
      }
    }

    // Compile Functions
    const runtime = { config: doc.config };
    if (doc.functions) {
      Object.entries(doc.functions).forEach(([name, fnStr]) => {
        runtime[name] = eval(`(${fnStr})`);
      });
    }
    if (runtime.init) runtime.init(run_doc);

    // Register under PK always, semantic alias when available
    globalThis[doc.doctype][doc.name] = runtime;
    if (semanticValue && semanticValue !== doc.name) {
      globalThis[doc.doctype][semanticValue] = runtime;
    }
  }

  this._invalidateIndex();
},

  // ─── Compile All ────────────────────────────────────────
compileAll: async function() {
  let compiled = 0;
  for (const run of Object.values(this.runs)) {
    const docs = run.target?.data;
    if (!Array.isArray(docs)) continue;
    
    const hasCompilable = docs.some(doc => doc?.functions || doc?.scripts);
    if (hasCompilable) {
      await this._compileDocument(run);
      compiled++;
    }
  }
  console.log(`✓ Compiled ${compiled} run(s)`);
  return compiled;
},

  // ─── handleField ──────────────────────────────────
  _handleField : function(fieldname, fieldtype, rootObj, path) {
    // Navigate to container (the data array/object)
    const parts = path.replace(/\[(\d+)\]/g, '.$1').split('.').filter(Boolean);
    let container = globalThis;
    
    for (const part of parts) {
      container = container[part];
      if (!container) throw new Error(`Invalid path: ${path}`);
    }
    
    // Container IS the data (array or object)
    const target = Array.isArray(container) ? container[0] : container;
    
    // ✅ Get doctype FROM the data object
    const doctype = target.doctype;
    if (!doctype) throw new Error(`No doctype in data at path: ${path}`);
    
    // ✅ Get schema from CW
    const schema = CW.Schema[doctype];
    if (!schema) throw new Error(`Schema not found: ${doctype}`);
    
    // ✅ Get field from schema
    const field = schema.fields.find(f => f.fieldname === fieldname);
    if (!field) throw new Error(`Field "${fieldname}" not in ${doctype} schema`);
    
    // Apply handler
    const handler = this._fieldHandlers[fieldtype || field.fieldtype];
    const value = handler 
      ? handler(target[fieldname], field, { rootObj, schema })
      : target[fieldname];
    
    target[fieldname] = value;
    return value;
  },

};

// ============================================================
// Wrap CW in Proxy for Dynamic Doctype Access
// ============================================================

globalThis.CW = new Proxy(globalThis.CW, {
  get(target, prop) {
    // Direct property/method access
    if (prop in target) return target[prop];
    
    // Dynamic doctype access: CW.Adapter, CW.User, etc.
    target._buildIndex();
    return target._index[prop] || {};
  }
});
5:37 PM

The compiler uses template literal:

javascript
runtime[name] = eval(`(${fnStr})`);

The problem is the col function contains a template literal with backticks inside:

javascript
"function(field) { if (field === 'id') return 'id'; return \"json_extract(data, '$.\" + field + \"')\"; }"

Wait, that's fine. Let me look more carefully — the issue is that fnStr gets interpolated into the template literal `(${fnStr})`. If fnStr contains a backtick, it breaks the outer template literal prematurely.

Check your col function — it uses \" which is fine. But other adapters like auth have template literals with backticks inside their function strings:

javascript
`user:${userAuth.id}`  // backtick in auth adapter
`SELECT id...`         // backtick in getUserWithCapabilities

When those get interpolated into eval(`(${fnStr})`), the inner backtick closes the outer template literal — Unexpected token '-' is exactly what you'd get.

The fix is in your compiler, change:

javascript
runtime[name] = eval(`(${fnStr})`);

to:

javascript
runtime[name] = eval('(' + fnStr + ')');

This avoids the template literal collision entirely. The other adapters happened to work because their backtick-containing functions weren't being hit, or the error was swallowed.

same error

5:39 PM

Can you share the exact error with the stack trace now? The line number in coworker-state.js would tell us exactly which function string is failing.

Also run this in console to find the culprit:

javascript
for (const run of Object.values(CW.runs)) {
  const docs = run.target?.data;
  if (!Array.isArray(docs)) continue;
  for (const doc of docs) {
    if (!doc?.functions) continue;
    for (const [name, fnStr] of Object.entries(doc.functions)) {
      try { eval('(' + fnStr + ')'); }
      catch(e) { console.log('FAIL:', doc.adapter_name || doc.name, name, e.message); }
    }
  }
}

This will tell us exactly which adapter and which function is breaking.

for (const run of Object.values(CW.runs)) { const docs = run.target?.data; if (!Array.isArray(docs)) continue; for (const doc of docs) { if (!doc?.functions) continue; for (const [name, fnStr] of Object.entries(doc.functions)) { try { eval('(' + fnStr + ')'); } catch(e) { console.log('FAIL:', doc.adapter_name || doc.name, name, e.message); } } } } VM906:8 FAIL: pocketbase insert Unexpected token '-' VM906:8 FAIL: pocketbase update missing ) after argument list VM906:8 FAIL: pocketbase delete missing ) after argument list

5:40 PM

Not your adapter — it's the PocketBase adapter that's broken. These three functions have invalid JS:

insert — this line is broken:

javascript
: new-${Date.now()}-${Math.random().toString(36).substr(2, 9)}

The - between new and ${} is being parsed as subtraction — new minus a template literal. It's missing quotes around the template literal.

update and delete — these have unquoted template literals too:

javascript
throw new Error(Record not found: ${identifier})  // missing quotes
filter: data.name = "${identifier}"               // broken syntax

These were broken before our session — nothing to do with the SQLite adapter. Fix them in PocketBase and compileAll() will work.

give me correct full cod of pocketbase adapter

5:40 PM
Done

Three fixes applied to the broken functions:

insert — replaced template literal with string concatenation:

javascript
// broken
: new-${Date.now()}-${Math.random()...}
// fixed  
: 'new-' + Date.now() + '-' + Math.random()...

update — fixed filter string and error message:

javascript
// broken
filter: data.name = "${identifier}"
throw new Error(Record not found: ${identifier})
// fixed
filter: 'data.name = "' + identifier + '"'
throw new Error('Record not found: ' + identifier)

delete — same filter and error fixes as update.

Update just the functions field on the adapterb7l0z4ur record in PocketBase with this file.

Pb functions
Code · JSON 

it works. question. Why its immediately try to connect after compile CW.compileAll() VM1464:1 PocketBase initialized: undefined coworker-state.js:194 ✓ Compiled 1 run(s) Promise {<fulfilled>: 1} VM1478:1 Fetch failed loading: POST "http://localhost:3000/". eval @ VM1478:1 eval @ VM1477:1 _compileDocument @ coworker-state.js:169 compileAll @ coworker-state.js:190 (anonymous) @ VM1457:1 VM1478:1 POST http://localhost:3000/ net::ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED eval @ VM1478:1 eval @ VM1477:1 _compileDocument @ coworker-state.js:169 compileAll @ coworker-state.js:190 (anonymous) @ VM1457:1Understand this error VM1478:1 Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: Failed to fetch eval @ VM1478:1 eval @ VM1477:1 _compileDocument @ coworker-state.js:169 compileAll @ coworker-state.js:190 (anonymous) @ VM1457:1Understand this error

5:42 PM

Because your init function runs immediately during compileAll():

javascript
// Compile Functions
const runtime = { config: doc.config };
if (doc.functions) {
  Object.entries(doc.functions).forEach(([name, fnStr]) => {
    runtime[name] = eval('(' + fnStr + ')');
  });
}
if (runtime.init) runtime.init(run_doc);  // ← runs immediately

Two problems:

  1. PocketBase init reads config from run_doc.target.data[0] but at compileAll() time run_doc is the original select run, not an init run — so config.url is undefined
  2. SQLite init immediately tries to create tables by calling runtime.sql() which fires a fetch to localhost:3000 — connection refused because your server isn't running yet, or isn't expected to run at compile time

Fix options:

A) Don't auto-call init in compileAll — remove that line and call it explicitly when you actually need it:

javascript
// if (runtime.init) runtime.init(run_doc); // remove

B) Make init lazy — only runs on first actual operation, not at compile time

C) Guard in init itself — check if conditions are met before connecting

Which do you prefer? Option A is simplest and matches how PocketBase SDK works — you initialize when you need it, not at class load time.

I took A, recompiled and started localhost:3000. whats next

5:46 PM

Test it. Run a create then a select:

javascript
// 1. Init the table first
await CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"].init()

// 2. Create a record
await CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"].create({
  target_doctype: "Task",
  input: {
    doctype: "Task",
    title: "Test task",
    status: "Open"
  }
})

// 3. Select it back
await CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"].select({
  source_doctype: "Task",
  query: { where: { status: "Open" } }
})

What do you get?

// 1. Init the table first await CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"].init() // 2. Create a record await CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"].create({ target_doctype: "Task", input: { doctype: "Task", title: "Test task", status: "Open" } }) // 3. Select it back await CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"].select({ source_doctype: "Task", query: { where: { status: "Open" } } }) VM1559:1 Fetch failed loading: POST "http://localhost:3000/". eval @ VM1559:1 eval @ VM1558:1 (anonymous) @ VM1576:2 VM1559:2 Uncaught TypeError: Failed to fetch at Object.eval [as sql] (eval at <anonymous> (coworker-state.js:166:25), <anonymous>:1:75) at Object.eval [as init] (eval at <anonymous> (coworker-state.js:166:25), <anonymous>:1:111) at <anonymous>:2:34

5:46 PM

Your server isn't running or isn't reachable. The fetch to http://localhost:3000/ is failing with connection refused.

Check:

bash
# Is the server running?
node index.js

Then verify it responds:

bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/ -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d "{\"query\":\"SELECT 1\",\"params\":[]}"

Is index.js running in a terminal?

C:\python\cosqlite>node index.js (node:24216) [MODULE_TYPELESS_PACKAGE_JSON] Warning: Module type of file:///C:/python/cosqlite/index.js is not specified and it doesn't parse as CommonJS. Reparsing as ES module because module syntax was detected. This incurs a performance overhead. To eliminate this warning, add "type": "module" to C:\python\cosqlite\package.json. (Use node --trace-warnings ... to show where the warning was created) Server listening on http://localhost:3000

5:47 PM

Server is running. The issue is the browser can't reach localhost:3000 from the page — this is a CORS problem. The browser blocks cross-origin requests unless the server sends CORS headers.

Add CORS to your index.js:

javascript
http.createServer(async (req, res) => {
  // Add CORS headers
  res.setHeader('Access-Control-Allow-Origin', '*');
  res.setHeader('Access-Control-Allow-Methods', 'POST, OPTIONS');
  res.setHeader('Access-Control-Allow-Headers', 'Content-Type');

  // Handle preflight
  if (req.method === 'OPTIONS') {
    res.writeHead(204);
    res.end();
    return;
  }

  if (req.method === 'POST') {
    const response = await handleRequest(req);
    res.writeHead(response.status, response.headers);
    res.end(response.body);
  } else {
    res.writeHead(404, { 'Content-Type': 'text/plain' });
    res.end('Not Found');
  }
}).listen(3000, () => console.log('Server listening on http://localhost:3000'));

Restart the server then try again.

await CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"].init() // 2. Create a record await CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"].create({ target_doctype: "Task", input: { doctype: "Task", title: "Test task", status: "Open" } }) // 3. Select it back await CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"].select({ source_doctype: "Task", query: { where: { status: "Open" } } }) VM1559:1 Fetch finished loading: POST "http://localhost:3000/". eval @ VM1559:1 eval @ VM1558:1 (anonymous) @ VM1580:2 VM1559:1 Fetch finished loading: POST "http://localhost:3000/". eval @ VM1559:1 eval @ VM1558:1 await in eval (anonymous) @ VM1580:2 VM1559:1 Fetch finished loading: POST "http://localhost:3000/". eval @ VM1559:1 eval @ VM1558:1 await in eval (anonymous) @ VM1580:2 VM1559:1 Fetch finished loading: POST "http://localhost:3000/". eval @ VM1559:1 eval @ VM1558:1 await in eval (anonymous) @ VM1580:2 VM1559:1 Fetch finished loading: POST "http://localhost:3000/". eval @ VM1559:1 eval @ VM1558:1 await in eval (anonymous) @ VM1580:2 VM1559:1 Fetch finished loading: POST "http://localhost:3000/". eval @ VM1559:1 eval @ VM1558:1 await in eval (anonymous) @ VM1580:2 VM1558:1 SQLite adapter ready: http://localhost:3000 VM1559:1 Fetch finished loading: POST "http://localhost:3000/". eval @ VM1559:1 eval @ VM1569:1 (anonymous) @ VM1580:5 VM1559:1 Fetch finished loading: POST "http://localhost:3000/". eval @ VM1559:1 eval @ VM1569:1 await in eval (anonymous) @ VM1580:5 VM1559:1 Fetch finished loading: POST "http://localhost:3000/". eval @ VM1559:1 eval @ VM1568:1 (anonymous) @ VM1580:15 {data: Array(1), meta: {…}}

5:48 PM

It works! 🎉

javascript
{data: Array(1), meta: {…}}

Record created and selected back successfully. The full pipeline is working:

  • init() — created table and indexes
  • create() — inserted the Task record
  • select() — queried it back with where: { status: "Open" }

Next step — wire it into your coworker.run() flow via options: { adapter: 'sqlite-local' }, or do you want to test more operations first?

lets check the RBAC.by generating user and Task _allow

5:49 PM

Good plan. To test RBAC we need:

  1. A User record with _allowed_read containing some role ids
  2. A Task record with _allowed and _allowed_read containing matching/non-matching role ids
  3. A select with run_doc.user set to simulate the JWT-resolved user

Let's create the test data. Run this step by step:

javascript
// Step 1: Create a role id (just a string, simulate generateId)
const roleProjectUser = "role-project-user";
const roleAdmin = "role-admin";

// Step 2: Create User record
await CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"].create({
  target_doctype: "User",
  input: {
    doctype: "User",
    name: "user-john",
    email: "john@test.com",
    _allowed_read: [roleProjectUser],  // John's capabilities
    _allowed: [],
    owner: ""
  }
})

// Step 3: Create Task John CAN read (matching role)
await CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"].create({
  target_doctype: "Task",
  input: {
    doctype: "Task",
    title: "Task John can read",
    status: "Open",
    _allowed_read: [roleProjectUser],  // matches John's capability
    _allowed: [roleAdmin],
    owner: ""
  }
})

// Step 4: Create Task John CANNOT read
await CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"].create({
  target_doctype: "Task",
  input: {
    doctype: "Task",
    title: "Task John cannot read",
    status: "Open",
    _allowed_read: [roleAdmin],  // John doesn't have this
    _allowed: [roleAdmin],
    owner: ""
  }
})

Then test the RBAC select:

javascript
// Should return only 1 task (the one John can read)
await CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"].select({
  source_doctype: "Task",
  query: { where: { status: "Open" } },
  user: {
    name: "user-john",
    _allowed_read: ["role-project-user"]
  }
})

Run it and share what comes back.

// Step 1: Create a role id (just a string, simulate generateId) const roleProjectUser = "role-project-user"; const roleAdmin = "role-admin"; // Step 2: Create User record await CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"].create({ target_doctype: "User", input: { doctype: "User", name: "user-john", email: "john@test.com", _allowed_read: [roleProjectUser], // John's capabilities _allowed: [], owner: "" } }) // Step 3: Create Task John CAN read (matching role) await CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"].create({ target_doctype: "Task", input: { doctype: "Task", title: "Task John can read", status: "Open", _allowed_read: [roleProjectUser], // matches John's capability _allowed: [roleAdmin], owner: "" } }) // Step 4: Create Task John CANNOT read await CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"].create({ target_doctype: "Task", input: { doctype: "Task", title: "Task John cannot read", status: "Open", _allowed_read: [roleAdmin], // John doesn't have this _allowed: [roleAdmin], owner: "" } }) VM1559:1 Fetch finished loading: POST "http://localhost:3000/". eval @ VM1559:1 eval @ VM1569:1 (anonymous) @ VM1584:6 VM1559:1 Fetch finished loading: POST "http://localhost:3000/". eval @ VM1559:1 eval @ VM1569:1 await in eval (anonymous) @ VM1584:6 VM1559:1 Fetch finished loading: POST "http://localhost:3000/". eval @ VM1559:1 eval @ VM1569:1 (anonymous) @ VM1584:19 VM1559:1 Fetch finished loading: POST "http://localhost:3000/". eval @ VM1559:1 eval @ VM1569:1 await in eval (anonymous) @ VM1584:19 VM1559:1 Fetch finished loading: POST "http://localhost:3000/". eval @ VM1559:1 eval @ VM1569:1 (anonymous) @ VM1584:32 VM1559:1 Fetch finished loading: POST "http://localhost:3000/". eval @ VM1559:1 eval @ VM1569:1 await in eval (anonymous) @ VM1584:32 {data: {…}, meta: {…}} // Should return only 1 task (the one John can read) await CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"].select({ source_doctype: "Task", query: { where: { status: "Open" } }, user: { name: "user-john", _allowed_read: ["role-project-user"] } }) VM1559:1 Fetch finished loading: POST "http://localhost:3000/". eval @ VM1559:1 eval @ VM1568:1 (anonymous) @ VM1588:2

5:50 PM

What did the select return? The output got cut off. Share the result object.

{ "data": [ { "doctype": "Task", "title": "Task John can read", "status": "Open", "_allowed_read": [ "role-project-user" ], "_allowed": [ "role-admin" ], "owner": "", "name": "task-1771455014660-q9y3db", "created": "2026-02-18T22:50:14.660Z", "updated": "2026-02-18T22:50:14.660Z" } ], "meta": { "total": 1, "page": 1, "pageSize": 1, "totalPages": 1, "hasMore": false } }

5:51 PM

🎉 RBAC works perfectly.

  • Returned exactly 1 record — "Task John can read"
  • "Task John cannot read" was filtered out by the SQL RBAC filter
  • Data is correctly unpacked — flat record with all fields visible

The full stack is working:

  • SQLite HTTP server ✅
  • Adapter compiled and callable via CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"] ✅
  • Create/Select ✅
  • RBAC filtering in pure SQL ✅
  • Data serialization/deserialization ✅

What's next — wire it into coworker.run() via options: { adapter: 'sqlite-local' }?

is this effective RBAC filtering

11:46 PM

Yes, it's effective. Here's why:

The RBAC filter runs in a single SQL query — no separate round trips to fetch user records. The user's capabilities come in via run_doc.user._allowed_read (already resolved from JWT) and get passed as a JSON parameter to json_each(). SQLite evaluates the intersection set inside the same query that fetches the data.

The json_each() JOIN approach is the correct way to do array intersection in SQLite. It's not doing LIKE '%role%' string matching which would be fragile — it's doing a proper value-level join between two JSON arrays.

Fail-closed is enforced — if _allowed_read and _allowed are both empty arrays, json_each() returns no rows, the EXISTS returns false, and the record is hidden. No accidental leaks.

One potential concern — json_each() on JSON arrays stored as strings requires SQLite 3.38+ for reliable behavior. Older SQLite versions had bugs with json_each() on nested structures. Since you're running on a modern Node.js with better-sqlite3, this should be fine — it bundles a recent SQLite version.

One real limitation — if a user has many roles (say 50+) and a table has millions of records, the json_each() JOIN could get expensive since there's no index on JSON array contents. For your use case this is likely fine, but worth knowing if scale becomes a concern later.

Overall — solid, production-worthy RBAC for your use case.

how effective is indexing

11:55 PM

For your current schema the indexes help with simple equality filters but not with RBAC.

What the indexes help with:

sql
-- These use indexes (fast)
json_extract(data, '$.doctype') = 'Task'    -- idx_doctype
json_extract(data, '$.name') = 'task-123'   -- idx_name
json_extract(data, '$.created') > '2026...' -- idx_created

SQLite functional indexes work well for equality and range queries on scalar values.

What the indexes don't help with:

sql
-- RBAC uses json_each() -- no index possible on array contents
EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM json_each(json_extract(data, '$._allowed_read')) rec
  JOIN json_each(?) usr ON usr.value = rec.value
)

There is no way to index inside a JSON array in SQLite. Every RBAC check does a full scan of the JSON array for each row that passes the doctype filter.

Practical impact:

  • Small tables (< 10k rows) — negligible, fast enough
  • Medium tables (10k-100k rows) — doctype index cuts the scan significantly, RBAC runs on the filtered subset only
  • Large tables (100k+) — could be slow if many rows share a doctype

If scale becomes a concern the mitigation is to denormalize — add a real column like allowed_roles TEXT and index it, then use LIKE or a junction table. But that sacrifices schema simplicity.

For your current use case — fine as is.

the question was about non-RBAC indexes for SELECT etc

11:56 PM

For regular SELECT queries the indexes are effective when SQLite can use them.

Effective cases:

sql
-- doctype filter -- always first, hits idx_doctype, eliminates most rows
WHERE json_extract(data, '$.doctype') = 'Task'

-- name lookup -- hits idx_name, essentially O(1)
WHERE json_extract(data, '$.name') = 'task-123'

-- date range -- hits idx_created
WHERE json_extract(data, '$.created') > '2026-01-01'

Ineffective cases:

sql
-- json_extract on non-indexed fields -- full table scan
WHERE json_extract(data, '$.status') = 'Open'
WHERE json_extract(data, '$.title') LIKE '%test%'

So your current query pattern:

javascript
{ where: { status: "Open" } }

This generates json_extract(data, '$.status') = ? — no index on status, full scan of all rows that passed the doctype filter.

Practical recommendation:

Add indexes for fields you filter on frequently. In your init function add:

javascript
await runtime.sql('CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_status ON ' + table + "(json_extract(data, '$.status'))");

Or better — make it data-structure driven. Your adapter config could include an indexes array:

json
"indexes": ["status", "docstatus", "assigned_to"]

And init creates them dynamically. That way each adapter instance defines its own indexes based on its data shape.

I always query by doctype first

11:56 PM

Then your indexing is effective.

Since doctype is always the first filter, idx_doctype cuts the working set down to just that doctype's rows first. All subsequent filters (status, title, etc.) run only against that reduced set — not the full table.

So the scan cost is proportional to rows per doctype, not total table size. If you have 100k rows but only 500 Tasks, the unindexed status filter only scans those 500. That's fast enough for most real-world use cases.

The only scenario where you'd need additional indexes is if a single doctype has tens of thousands of rows and you filter on a high-cardinality field frequently. For your use case — not a concern.

another question over concurrency

12:13 AM

Go ahead — what's the question?

how good is this setup for concurrent updates of 1 row

12:13 AM

Not great. SQLite is a file-based database with a single writer lock. Here's the reality:

What happens with concurrent updates to 1 row:

SQLite uses database-level write locking — only one write at a time. If two requests try to update the same row simultaneously, one waits for the other to finish. By default better-sqlite3 will throw SQLITE_BUSY if the lock isn't released within the timeout.

Your specific setup adds another layer:

Your update does 3 sequential operations per row:

javascript
// 1. SELECT to fetch existing
// 2. merge in JS
// 3. UPDATE with merged data
```

This is a read-modify-write pattern with no transaction wrapping it. Under concurrent access:
```
Request A: SELECT row  → gets { status: "Open" }
Request B: SELECT row  → gets { status: "Open" }
Request A: UPDATE row  → sets { status: "Done" }
Request B: UPDATE row  → sets { status: "Open" }  ← overwrites A

Classic lost update problem.

For your localhost dev setup — not a concern. Single user, sequential requests.

If this ever goes to production with real concurrency the fixes are:

  1. Wrap select+update in a transaction in your server's _sql handler
  2. Add optimistic locking via an updated timestamp check
  3. Or accept last-write-wins since your data model may not require strict consistency

What's your intended deployment scenario?

if last wins?

12:15 AM

Then your current setup is fine as-is.

Last-write-wins means you accept that under concurrent updates the final state is whatever the last request wrote. For many real-world scenarios this is perfectly acceptable — task status updates, document edits, settings changes. The data is never corrupted, just potentially stale for a brief moment.

Your current flow naturally implements last-write-wins:

javascript
// A writes status=Done at T1
// B writes status=Open at T2  
// Result: status=Open  ← last writer wins, no crash, no corruption

SQLite's write lock actually helps here — it serializes the writes so you never get partial writes or torn rows. The "lost update" is intentional, not accidental corruption.

The only case where last-write-wins becomes a problem is financial or inventory data where every update must be cumulative — like incrementing a counter. For that you'd need UPDATE SET count = count + 1 in SQL rather than read-modify-write in JS. But for your document-style data it's fine.

any perf loss of modifying single record

12:15 AM

Yes, one real perf cost — your update reads the full row first, merges in JS, then writes the full data JSON blob back:

javascript
// 1. SELECT * → deserialize entire data blob
// 2. Object.assign merge in JS
// 3. JSON.stringify entire record → UPDATE

So for a record with a large data blob (say 50 fields), updating a single field like status still serializes and writes all 50 fields back. The cost scales with record size, not change size.

For typical document sizes (under 10KB of JSON) this is negligible. SQLite handles it fine.

Only becomes a concern if:

  • Records are very large (MB-scale blobs)
  • Updates are extremely frequent (thousands/second)

For your use case — not a concern.

any blocking ?

12:16 AM

Yes. better-sqlite3 is synchronous — every SQL call blocks the Node.js event loop for its duration.

Your HTTP server is async but the actual SQLite operations underneath are blocking:

javascript
// This blocks the event loop until SQLite finishes
const stmt = this.db.prepare(stmtText);
stmt.all(params);  // synchronous, blocking

Practical impact:

  • Single fast query (< 1ms) — effectively no blocking, unnoticeable
  • Slow query or large result set — blocks all other incoming requests for that duration
  • Concurrent requests — they queue up, each waiting for the previous to finish

For localhost single-user dev — zero concern.

If you ever expose this server to multiple users — consider switching to better-sqlite3's WAL mode which helps read concurrency, or move to node:sqlite (Node 22+ built-in) which has async support, or wrap in a worker thread.

For now your use case is local dev tool — synchronous blocking SQLite is the right tradeoff. Simple, fast, no dependencies beyond better-sqlite3.

why wal helps here

12:17 AM

WAL (Write-Ahead Log) helps specifically with read/write concurrency, not write/write.

In default journal mode:

  • A writer blocks ALL readers
  • Readers block writers

In WAL mode:

  • Writers and readers don't block each other
  • Multiple readers can run simultaneously with a concurrent writer
  • Only write/write still serializes

So for your pattern — one user updating while another is selecting — WAL eliminates that blocking. Reads see the last committed version while a write is in progress.

Enable it with one line in your server startup:

javascript
db.pragma('journal_mode = WAL');

For your current single-user local setup it makes no difference. But it's essentially free to enable and good practice.

when it might be needed

12:18 AM

WAL becomes relevant when you move this beyond a single local dev tool — specifically:

Multiple browser tabs hitting the same local server simultaneously — one tab writing while another is reading.

Shared local server — if you expose the SQLite server on your LAN and multiple team members hit it at the same time.

Background sync — if you add a background process that periodically writes (sync, cleanup, indexing) while the UI is actively reading.

Any production deployment — if this ever runs as a real backend with multiple users.

For your current setup — one browser, one server, sequential requests — WAL adds nothing. But since it's one pragma line with no downside, worth enabling now so you never have to think about it again.

Done

Two good real-world examples that use JSON in a SQLite cell with significant stars:

1. PocketBase (~45k stars) The closest to your exact pattern. Stores records in SQLite with a data JSON column, uses json_extract for filtering, and supports generated column indexes. It's literally the backend your PocketBase adapter talks to — and it validated the same architectural choice you made independently. https://github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase

2. Datasette (~10k stars) by Simon Willison A Python tool for exploring SQLite databases. Heavily uses json_extract for querying JSON stored in text columns, and its companion sqlite-utils library uses the same single-table JSON blob pattern for importing and querying arbitrary data. The author has written extensively about using SQLite as a document database with json_extract indexes. https://github.com/simonw/datasette

Both validate your approach — the pattern of id TEXT PRIMARY KEY + data TEXT + json_extract functional indexes is well-established in production tools with large user bases, not just a local experiment.

2 / 2

give detailed decription of all implementation esp., how adapter works, what convertion into sql does etc

8:07 AM
Done
2 / 2

give HERE (not with additional skill) gie detailed decription of all implementation esp., how adapter works, what convertion into sql does etc

8:10 AM

Here's the full detailed description of the entire implementation.

Coworker SQLite Adapter — Complete Implementation Reference

Overview

The system stores all application documents in a single SQLite table with a minimal schema. Every record — regardless of doctype — lives in the same table. The adapter is itself stored as a document in PocketBase, fetched at runtime, compiled into live functions, and registered on globalThis. No code is bundled or deployed — the adapter is data.

1. Storage Schema

sql
CREATE TABLE item (
  id   TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  data TEXT DEFAULT '{}'
)

Two columns only. id is the primary key — a string like task-1771455014660-q9y3db. data is a JSON string containing every field of the document including doctype, name, owner, _allowed, _allowed_read, and all application fields.

A stored row looks like:

json
{
  "id": "task-1771455014660-q9y3db",
  "data": "{\"doctype\":\"Task\",\"name\":\"task-1771455014660-q9y3db\",\"title\":\"Test task\",\"status\":\"Open\",\"owner\":\"user-john\",\"_allowed\":[\"role-admin\"],\"_allowed_read\":[\"role-project-user\"],\"created\":\"2026-02-18T22:50:14.660Z\",\"updated\":\"2026-02-18T22:50:14.660Z\"}"
}

From the application's perspective this never exists — unpack() always deserializes it into a flat object before returning. The data wrapper is purely a storage implementation detail.

2. Functional Indexes

Created once during init():

sql
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_name     ON item(json_extract(data, '$.name'));
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_doctype  ON item(json_extract(data, '$.doctype'));
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_docstatus ON item(json_extract(data, '$.docstatus'));
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_created  ON item(json_extract(data, '$.created'));
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_updated  ON item(json_extract(data, '$.updated'));

SQLite functional indexes extract a value from the JSON blob and index it as if it were a real column. When a query filters on json_extract(data, '$.doctype') = 'Task', SQLite uses idx_doctype rather than scanning all rows. Since every query starts with a doctype filter, this index is the primary cost-reduction mechanism — it reduces the working set from the full table to just rows of that doctype before any other filtering runs.

3. HTTP Transport Layer

The adapter does not talk to SQLite directly — it sends HTTP POST requests to a local Node.js server:

javascript
// sql() function
const res = await fetch(runtime.config.url, {
  method: 'POST',
  headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
  body: JSON.stringify({ query: query, params: params })
});
const results = await res.json();
return Array.isArray(results[0]) ? results[0] : results;

The server at http://localhost:3000 receives { query, params }, executes the SQL via better-sqlite3, and returns rows as JSON. This indirection exists because the adapter runs in the browser — it cannot import Node modules. The HTTP boundary separates browser JS from the Node.js SQLite engine cleanly. CORS headers on the server allow the browser to reach localhost.

4. The runtime Object

When compileAll() processes the adapter document, it builds a runtime object:

javascript
const runtime = { config: doc.config };
Object.entries(doc.functions).forEach(([name, fnStr]) => {
  runtime[name] = eval('(' + fnStr + ')');
});
globalThis.Adapter["sqlite-local"] = runtime;

runtime is a plain object containing config plus all 15 compiled functions. Every function references runtime directly by closure — because runtime is declared in the outer scope of _compileDocument, all eval'd functions close over it. This is why functions call each other as runtime.whereToSQL(...), runtime.sql(...) etc. rather than this.method(). The object is registered on globalThis.Adapter["sqlite-local"] and also accessible via CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"] through the Proxy-based index.

5. Query Translation — whereToSQL()

This is the core of the adapter. It accepts a Prisma-style where object and produces a { sql, params } pair for use in a prepared statement.

Simple equality:

javascript
{ status: "Open" }
// → json_extract(data, '$.status') = ?    params: ["Open"]

Comparison operators:

javascript
{ age: { gt: 30 } }
// → json_extract(data, '$.age') > ?    params: [30]

{ price: { gte: 100, lte: 500 } }
// → json_extract(data, '$.price') >= ? AND json_extract(data, '$.price') <= ?
//   params: [100, 500]

String matching:

javascript
{ title: { contains: "test" } }
// → json_extract(data, '$.title') LIKE ?    params: ["%test%"]

{ name: { startsWith: "task-" } }
// → json_extract(data, '$.name') LIKE ?    params: ["task-%"]

Array membership:

javascript
{ status: { in: ["Open", "In Progress"] } }
// → json_extract(data, '$.status') IN (?,?)    params: ["Open","In Progress"]

Null check:

javascript
{ assignedTo: null }
// → json_extract(data, '$.assignedTo') IS NULL

Logical operators:

javascript
{ OR: [{ status: "Open" }, { status: "Draft" }] }
// → (json_extract(data,'$.status') = ? OR json_extract(data,'$.status') = ?)
//   params: ["Open","Draft"]

{ AND: [{ status: "Open" }, { priority: "High" }] }
// → (json_extract(data,'$.status') = ? AND json_extract(data,'$.priority') = ?)

{ NOT: { status: "Closed" } }
// → NOT (json_extract(data,'$.status') = ?)

All values go through parameterized queries — ? placeholders with values in the params array. SQLite substitutes them safely, preventing SQL injection entirely.

The col() helper centralizes field-to-SQL mapping:

javascript
col('id')     → 'id'                              // raw column
col('status') → "json_extract(data, '$.status')"  // JSON path

6. RBAC Filtering — rbacFilter()

RBAC is implemented entirely in SQL using json_each() to intersect JSON arrays. It generates a SQL fragment appended to every query's WHERE clause.

Read operations — a record is visible if any of:

  • User is the owner: json_extract(data, '$.owner') = ?
  • Record is public: _allowed_read contains 'roleispublic'
  • User's capabilities ∩ record's _allowed is non-empty
  • User's capabilities ∩ record's _allowed_read is non-empty
sql
json_extract(data, '$.owner') = ?
OR EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM json_each(json_extract(data, '$._allowed_read')) r
  WHERE r.value = 'roleispublic'
)
OR EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM json_each(json_extract(data, '$._allowed')) rec
  JOIN json_each(?) usr ON usr.value = rec.value
)
OR EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM json_each(json_extract(data, '$._allowed_read')) rec
  JOIN json_each(?) usr ON usr.value = rec.value
)

Write operations — only owner or _allowed match, no _allowed_read:

sql
json_extract(data, '$.owner') = ?
OR EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM json_each(json_extract(data, '$._allowed')) rec
  JOIN json_each(?) usr ON usr.value = rec.value
)

The user's capabilities array is passed as a JSON string parameter (e.g. '["role-project-user"]'). json_each(?) turns that string into a virtual table of rows. The JOIN checks for value intersection — if any role in the user's capabilities matches any role in the record's permissions, EXISTS returns true.

This runs as a single SQL query. No separate permission lookup, no round trips. The user capabilities are resolved from JWT once at request start and passed in run_doc.user._allowed_read.

7. buildWhere() — Assembling the Full WHERE Clause

All filter components are assembled here:

javascript
function buildWhere(run_doc) {
  // 1. Doctype filter (uses idx_doctype index)
  if (source_doctype && source_doctype !== 'All') {
    parts.push("json_extract(data, '$.doctype') = ?");
    params.push(source_doctype);
  }
  // 2. Application query filters
  if (query.where) {
    const w = runtime.whereToSQL(query.where);
    if (w.sql) { parts.push('(' + w.sql + ')'); params = params.concat(w.params); }
  }
  // 3. RBAC filter (appended to every query when user is present)
  if (user) {
    const r = runtime.rbacFilter(user, run_doc.operation);
    parts.push('(' + r.sql + ')');
    params = params.concat(r.params);
  }
  return { sql: parts.join(' AND '), params };
}

All three components are AND'd together. The doctype filter always comes first, runs against the index, and reduces the working set before RBAC and application filters touch any rows.

8. select() — Full Query Execution

javascript
async function select(run_doc) {
  const where = runtime.buildWhere(run_doc);       // WHERE clause + params
  const sort  = runtime.buildSort(query.orderBy);  // ORDER BY clause
  const built = runtime.selectSQL(where, sort, take, skip); // full SQL

  const rows = await runtime.sql(built.sql, built.params);  // HTTP → SQLite
  const data = rows.map(r => runtime.unpack(r));            // deserialize

  // If paginating, get total count separately
  if (take !== undefined) {
    const countSQL = 'SELECT COUNT(*) as count FROM item' + 
                     (where ? ' WHERE ' + where.sql : '');
    const countRows = await runtime.sql(countSQL, where ? where.params : []);
    total = countRows[0].count;
  }

  return { data, meta: { total, page, pageSize, totalPages, hasMore } };
}

For paginated queries this issues two SQL calls: one for the page of data, one for the total count. The count query reuses the same WHERE clause and params — it only omits LIMIT/OFFSET.

unpack() converts each raw SQLite row { id, data } into a flat application object by parsing the data JSON string. The id column is discarded since id is also stored inside the JSON.

9. create() — Insert

javascript
async function create(run_doc) {
  const id     = inputData.name || runtime.generateId(run_doc.target_doctype);
  const record = Object.assign({}, inputData, {
    name: id,
    doctype: inputData.doctype || run_doc.target_doctype,
    created: now,
    updated: now
  });
  await runtime.sql(
    'INSERT INTO item (id, data) VALUES (?, ?)',
    [id, JSON.stringify(record)]
  );
  const rows = await runtime.sql('SELECT * FROM item WHERE id = ?', [id]);
  return { data: runtime.unpack(rows[0]), meta: { operation: 'create', id } };
}

ID generation: generateId('Task') → task-1771455014660-q9y3db — doctype prefix + timestamp + 6-char random suffix. The entire record is serialized to JSON and stored in the data column. The insert is followed by an immediate SELECT to return the canonical stored form.

10. update() — Read-Modify-Write

javascript
async function update(run_doc) {
  // 1. SELECT matching rows (respects RBAC via buildWhere)
  const existingRows = await runtime.sql(built.sql, built.params);

  // 2. For each row: merge → write back
  for (const row of existingRows) {
    const existing = runtime.unpack(row);
    const merged   = Object.assign({}, existing, inputData, {
      name:    existing.name,    // preserve PK
      doctype: existing.doctype, // preserve doctype
      updated: new Date().toISOString()
    });
    await runtime.sql(
      'UPDATE item SET data = ? WHERE id = ?',
      [JSON.stringify(merged), row.id]
    );
  }
}

This is a last-write-wins read-modify-write. The merge preserves name and doctype (structural fields that must not change) and overwrites all other fields from inputData. Multiple matching rows are updated in a loop — update by query, not by id, is supported natively.

11. delete() — Guard + Batch Delete

javascript
async function delete(run_doc) {
  // Safety guard — requires explicit where clause
  if (!query.where || Object.keys(query.where).length === 0)
    throw new Error('DELETE requires query.where to prevent mass deletion');

  const rows = await runtime.sql(built.sql, built.params); // find matching
  for (const row of rows) {
    await runtime.sql('DELETE FROM item WHERE id = ?', [row.id]);
  }
}
```

The guard prevents accidental full-table deletes. Like update, it selects matching rows first (respecting RBAC) then deletes by id one at a time, ensuring no record outside the user's permissions is touched.

---

### 12. Adapter Lifecycle
```
PocketBase → fetch Adapter docs → CW.runs populated
    ↓
CW.compileAll()
    ↓
_compileDocument() for each run with functions/scripts
    ↓
runtime = { config: {...} }
eval('(' + fnStr + ')') for each function → runtime.fnName = compiled fn
    ↓
globalThis.Adapter["adapteriesop4rd"] = runtime
globalThis.Adapter["sqlite-local"]    = runtime  (semantic alias)
    ↓
CW._invalidateIndex()  → next CW.Adapter access rebuilds index
    ↓
CW.Adapter["sqlite-local"].select(run_doc)  → live execution

The adapter is fetched as data, compiled into live functions at runtime, and registered under both its PK (adapteriesop4rd) and its semantic alias (sqlite-local) derived from the adapter_name field via the Schema's autoname: "field:adapter_name" mapping.

Please describe how security with _allow works

8:14 AM

Security with _allowed and _allowed_read

Core Concept

Every document has two permission arrays stored inside its data JSON blob:

  • _allowed — who can read and write this document
  • _allowed_read — who can only read this document

Every user has one array resolved from their JWT:

  • user._allowed_read — the set of role IDs this user holds

These are just arrays of strings — role identifiers like "role-admin", "role-project-user". No separate roles table, no joins to a permissions system. The intersection check happens entirely in SQL at query time.

Where Permissions Live

Stored inside the document itself:

json
{
  "doctype": "Task",
  "name": "task-123",
  "title": "Fix bug",
  "owner": "user-john",
  "_allowed": ["role-admin"],
  "_allowed_read": ["role-project-user", "role-viewer"]
}

And on the user object passed in every run_doc:

javascript
run_doc.user = {
  name: "user-john",
  _allowed_read: ["role-project-user"]  // resolved from JWT
}

The Four Access Rules

Rule 1 — Owner always has full access:

sql
json_extract(data, '$.owner') = ?   -- params: [user.name]

The user who created the document always sees and can edit it, regardless of any role assignments.

Rule 2 — Public documents are readable by anyone:

sql
EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM json_each(json_extract(data, '$._allowed_read')) r
  WHERE r.value = 'roleispublic'
)

If _allowed_read contains the special string 'roleispublic', any user (even unauthenticated) can read it.

Rule 3 — _allowed grants read+write:

sql
EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 
  FROM json_each(json_extract(data, '$._allowed')) rec
  JOIN json_each(?) usr ON usr.value = rec.value
)
-- params: [JSON.stringify(user._allowed_read)]

If any role in the user's capabilities appears in the document's _allowed array, the user has full read+write access.

Rule 4 — _allowed_read grants read only:

sql
EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 
  FROM json_each(json_extract(data, '$._allowed_read')) rec
  JOIN json_each(?) usr ON usr.value = rec.value
)
-- params: [JSON.stringify(user._allowed_read)]
```
Same intersection check but only for read operations.

---

### How the Intersection Works in SQL

The user's capabilities are passed as a JSON string parameter — e.g. `'["role-project-user","role-viewer"]'`. `json_each(?)` turns that string into a virtual table:
```
value
─────────────────
role-project-user
role-viewer

json_each(json_extract(data, '$._allowed_read')) does the same for the document's permission array. The JOIN ON usr.value = rec.value finds matching rows. EXISTS returns true if any match is found — meaning the user holds at least one role that the document grants access to.

Read vs Write Decision

rbacFilter() checks the operation and applies different SQL:

javascript
const isWrite = ['update', 'delete', 'create'].indexOf(operation) !== -1;
```

**Read** (`select`, `takeone`) — all four rules apply:
```
owner  OR  public  OR  _allowed match  OR  _allowed_read match
```

**Write** (`create`, `update`, `delete`) — only two rules apply:
```
owner  OR  _allowed match
```

`_allowed_read` never grants write access. A user with only read roles cannot modify documents even if they can see them.

---

### Concrete Example

Documents in DB:
```
Task A: owner="user-john",  _allowed=["role-admin"],  _allowed_read=["role-project-user"]
Task B: owner="user-jane",  _allowed=["role-admin"],  _allowed_read=["role-admin"]
Task C: owner="user-jane",  _allowed=["role-admin"],  _allowed_read=["roleispublic"]
```

User John with `_allowed_read: ["role-project-user"]` does a select:
```
Task A → owner match (user-john) → ✅ visible
Task B → no owner, no public, role-admin not in John's caps → ❌ hidden
Task C → no owner, roleispublic in _allowed_read → ✅ visible (public)
```

John tries to update Task C:
```
Task C → no owner match, _allowed=["role-admin"], John has role-project-user → ❌ denied

The WHERE clause simply returns no rows for Task C on an update query — the update silently affects 0 records. No error thrown, no information leaked about whether the record exists.

Fail-Closed Behavior

If _allowed and _allowed_read are both empty arrays or missing, json_each() returns no rows, EXISTS returns false, owner check fails (different user), public check fails — the record is invisible. Empty permissions = no access by default. There is no "grant all" fallback.

What It Does NOT Cover

  • Field-level security — a user either sees the whole document or nothing. No per-field redaction.
  • Row-level audit logging — access decisions are not recorded.
  • Token validation — run_doc.user is assumed to be already resolved from a verified JWT before reaching the adapter. The adapter trusts whatever user object is passed in. JWT verification is the auth adapter's responsibility upstream.
  • Capability escalation prevention — if run_doc.user._allowed_read is tampered with client-side before reaching the adapter, RBAC is bypassed. The auth adapter must ensure user capabilities are loaded server-side from the DB, never taken from the client request body.


Claude is AI and can make mistakes. Please double-check responses.
